\nGood. The iron chapter narrative already cites \"ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 2014\" for the 1 mg/L chronic freshwater aquatic life standard. Let me verify these sources with a web search to confirm accuracy before the user adds them to the regulatory values file.\n
\n\nNow let me verify the sources. The chapter text already cites ADEC 2008 and USEPA 2014 for 1 mg/L — let me confirm those.\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\nNow I have good information to share. Let me check the ADEC 18 AAC 70 specifically for the iron standard. The search results show some ADEC documents but don't specifically mention iron at 1 mg/L in those.\n\nKey findings:\n1. **USEPA**: The 1 mg/L (1000 µg/L) chronic aquatic life criterion for iron is indeed a real standard — but it originates from USEPA's **1976** \"Red Book\" (Quality Criteria for Water), not a 2014 document. Multiple sources confirm this: \"The current USEPA chronic iron criterion of 1000 µg/L (total recoverable) for protection of aquatic life was adopted in 1976.\" The iron chapter text currently cites \"USEPA, 2014\" which needs to be checked.\n\n\n2. **ADEC**: I need to verify whether ADEC explicitly lists iron at 1 mg/L in 18 AAC 70 or if they're referencing the USEPA criterion instead. The existing narrative cites both \"ADEC, 2008\" and \"USEPA, 2014,\" so I should check what ADEC actually adopted.\n3. Iron doesn't appear on the USEPA's national recommended water quality criteria table as a priority pollutant, but the 1976 Red Book standard of 1000 µg/L has been widely adopted across states like Iowa and Oregon, so this is a legitimate criterion even if it's not in the current priority list.\n\nThe 1 mg/L value itself is correct and commonly used, but I'm concerned about whether the \"USEPA 2014\" citation is accurate—the criterion actually traces back to 1976, and I haven't found evidence of a 2014 EPA document specifically revising iron standards. Let me verify what ADEC actually specifies in their regulations. I need to be transparent about what I can actually verify versus what requires further checking—the ADEC 2008 and USEPA 2014 citations in the chapter might refer to specific documents rather than the regulatory codes themselves, so I should flag that the 1976 origin of the USEPA criterion doesn't necessarily contradict those citations without seeing the actual sources. the existing chapter text more carefully, this chapter was written for the 2016 report. The citations (ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 2014) were presumably verified by whoever wrote the 2016 report. The 1 mg/L value itself is widely cited and appears correct.\n\nMy recommendation should be:\n1. The 1 mg/L value is well-supported\n2. The USEPA citation should probably be USEPA 1976 (the original Red Book criterion) rather than 2014 — needs verification\n3. The ADEC 2008 reference is plausible — could be an Alaska-specific document or cite to 18 AAC 70 standards from around that time\n4. Before adding to all_reg_vals.csv, Ben should verify the specific ADEC citation\n\nSo the 1 mg/L threshold is solid and ready to add to the plot, but I should flag that the USEPA citation likely points to 1976 rather than 2014, and Ben needs to confirm whether ADEC's standard is explicitly stated in 18 AAC 70 or if it adopts the USEPA criterion by reference.\n
\n\nHere's what the research shows. I'll separate the threshold value from the citation question, because there's a discrepancy worth flagging.\n\n**The 1 mg/L value itself is well-supported.** The USEPA chronic iron criterion of 1,000 µg/L (1.0 mg/L, total recoverable) for protection of aquatic life was adopted in 1976 and is largely based on field observations of an iron-polluted Colorado stream in which trout and other fishes were absent at iron concentrations above that level. Most states adopted this EPA-recommended criterion of 1 mg/L (total iron) as the chronic criterion.\n\n**However, the citation in `iron.qmd` needs a closer look.** The chapter currently cites \"USEPA, 2014\" — but the USEPA iron criterion dates to 1976, not 2014. In 1999, EPA announced its intent to revise aquatic life criteria for iron, but no revised national criterion was published. Iron is a non-priority pollutant and does **not** appear on EPA's current National Recommended Water Quality Criteria table. So \"USEPA 2014\" may be a citation error from the 2016 report — the correct USEPA source is likely the 1976 \"Red Book\" (Quality Criteria for Water, USEPA 1976).\n\n**For the ADEC side**, I was not able to directly confirm an explicit iron entry in 18 AAC 70 from the search results. The ADEC 2008 citation in the chapter is plausible (could refer to a guidance document rather than the regulation itself), but you'd want to verify that independently.\n\n**Bottom line before touching `all_reg_vals.csv`:**\n- The **1 mg/L threshold value** is correct and appropriate to add.\n- The **\"USEPA, 2014\" citation** in `iron.qmd` line 14 should probably be corrected to \"USEPA, 1976\" — but you should confirm what document \"2014\" was intended to reference.\n- The **ADEC 2008 citation** should be verified against your copy of that document or 18 AAC 70.\n\nDo you want to proceed with adding 1 mg/L to `all_reg_vals.csv` now (subject to the citation fix separately), or would you prefer to resolve the citations first?\n\n\n" ] }, "lmMessage": [ { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "The user wants to verify the 1 mg/L Iron threshold suggestion from a previous session. Let me search for the current sources on this. The AGENTS.md mentioned:\n\n\"**Iron:** 1 mg/L — ADEC and USEPA chronic freshwater aquatic life (ADEC 2008, USEPA 2014). Add as new standard type e.g. `aquatic_life_chronic`.\"\n\nLet me look at what's currently in the iron chapter and the reg_vals file to understand what's there, and also do a web search to verify the sources.\n\nLet me also check the iron chapter and the existing regulatory values file.", "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "signature": "EtIFClsIDBgCKkAriuvqpD4XGCz96Ic4Fay7tqMSlvrbkgqtuQ/BZRQ8FiD9Z6m27195vNJEAwIB/Z8i2mAOuEGYMdVG3+fsU8+DMhFjbGF1ZGUtc29ubmV0LTQtNjgAEgwnxsnnlatcuBcqLgoaDIPdTWyQj2y+7M228yIwZgRIlwwiSmyWd7+jXCr4wEssxNyYEu8PtHI5Tb3rN+e9PeTu+im2l9uK4gveMrUAKqQEdlVGJ8uM2A89fdhIFJ2khAqQwpxGha2Fj2wlsIyrBOxdb9M4J0tXZOvJ6RMTBT6h35LWqlbEwvz2Uhc0ezIlUW0/yLZ1JCHAE9DkIEAIUI0zneJD174feDDmTNBRnYwyLJikzVl0ERA0yaq0ZB9x5cCQ1vxKpuiRFsw14vSN25Sk7McvAEdSpL/DFVHmXD/fljfe6TfsWn8wQqY4Qw6VefTrJ86Gy5Nt0vY6KdOAmTBENI1tgIWR0zLNMvjTKZEufJa7RMfXpPrPgVQPFDP/gyBfGYB+eDshZUi9RYXmS4SwsDBILbwJMO4+q8kBDUrbkdGcWcxOjvSaEhFhZQJyC4XcGZ767GUFyG3zvcXqsPYsu9eKELT/ciE6VkIDWKZ9VvcpfFJ4GLZS0KZ8KAYg2ViOg+NDwcbnxokJUVg0EGblYVFNMt3NVAFxQkxjptYV+fbn6DMKoVol7M+441LxmNdwrI1lRS5N7FbNeh/NenbBtf9FvmKDYDM4e7FpW/QBWyhKS7rlj+Y4aRrts4ZslttdIC5W5NfqtKs9oPnb3mQdbj476w0MvypYm3MMj9czUamh4MfQ7gtYRJHU97lAs0XugEgaSR3NrMEu6vWQ1elyeznCuvGKnTq/z/RDHxUKPZYpecnikmFqTTrsyFT+BQCvt4Z8SR0QjTyhSEDybGlmP3hkgvl9vLOHwKioBxWdbTXNMoVHSDXOtcfjgom1zUCk0K4YAQ==" } } }, { "type": "text", "text": "Good question — let me verify the sources before touching anything. I'll look at the current Iron chapter and the regulatory values file simultaneously." }, { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_01VgGZf3LfNW1twQcHTCMr3d", "toolName": "bash", "input": { "command": "cat -n D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx/parameters/iron.qmd" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } }, { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_01NUevZozZEvS3W8jQoUBsD5", "toolName": "bash", "input": { "command": "cat -n D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx/other/output/regulatory_values/all_reg_vals.csv" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 3, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 1874, "cache_read_input_tokens": 25260, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 1874, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 324, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 1874, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null }, "positai": { "timestamp": 1774993212683, "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6", "modelName": "Claude Sonnet 4.6", "providerId": "positai", "usage": { "inputTokens": 3, "outputTokens": 324, "totalTokens": 27461, "cacheReadTokens": 25260, "cacheWriteTokens": 1874, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 1874, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01VgGZf3LfNW1twQcHTCMr3d", "toolName": "bash", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "1\t# Iron\r\n 2\t\r\n 3\t---\r\n 4\texecute:\r\n 5\t echo: false\r\n 6\tdate: \"`r Sys.Date()`\"\r\n 7\tformat:\r\n 8\t html:\r\n 9\t code-fold: true\r\n 10\t code-tools: true\r\n 11\t code-summary: \"Show the code\"\r\n 12\t---\r\n 13\t\r\n 14\tNaturally present in many rocks and soils, iron is required by plants and animals for metabolism (Glass, 2001). Sources of detrimental levels of iron are industrial waste, mining, and iron-rich groundwater, and when high concentrations of iron react with dissolved oxygen, precipitates form that can harm salmon eggs and other aquatic life (USEPA, 1976). The ADEC and the USEPA have set the iron standard for the chronic exposure of freshwater aquatic life at 1 mg/L (see Appendix X) (ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 2014).\r\n 15\t\r\n 16\tIn the mainstem, the highest concentration of iron was 128 mg/L, which occurred at Mile 6.5 during spring 2006, and 0.03 mg/L was the lowest concentration that occurred at Mile 70 during spring 2013 (Table X). Mile 1.5 and Mile 6.5 had the highest medians during the spring and the summer (Figures X & X). Mile 1.5 and Mile 6.5 both exceeded the standard in the majority of samples (Table 13). In the spring, the median at Mile 10.1 also exceeded the standard, but the medians from all other mainstem locations were below the standard in both the spring and the summer (Figures X & X). There was a general upward trend in iron concentration from Kenai Lake to the estuary, especially in the summer.\r\n 17\t\r\n 18\tThe concentrations in the tributaries ranged from a high of 20.5 mg/L in Beaver Creek in spring 2006 to Russian River, which had the lowest concentration at below the MDL of 0.0027 mg/L (Table X). No Name Creek, Beaver Creek, Slikok Creek, Soldotna Creek, Funny River and Moose River all had medians exceeding the standard in Spring (Figure 94). No Name Creek and Beaver Creek had medians exceeding the standards during summer (Figure X). Russian River had the lowest iron levels with most of the samples reported below the MDL or MRL. In both the tributaries and the mainstem, iron levels were higher in the spring than in the summer (Figures X-X).\r\n 19\t\r\n 20\t```{r echo = F, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,fig.height=8, fig.width=10}\r\n 21\t\r\n 22\t# set parameter\r\n 23\tcharacteristic <- \"Iron\"\r\n 24\t\r\n 25\t# Define the sample fraction filter\r\n 26\tsample_fraction <- c(\"Total Recoverable\", \"Unfiltered\")\r\n 27\t\r\n 28\t# source static regulatory criteria boxplot function\r\n 29\tsource(\"functions/static_boxplot_function.R\")\r\n 30\t\r\n 31\t# Render plots directly in chunk — htmlwidgets must be output from direct chunk code in Quarto; printing inside source() is not captured for widget output.\r\n 32\tif (knitr::is_html_output()) {\r\n 33\t htmltools::tagList(\r\n 34\t plotly::ggplotly(plots$tributary_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 700) |> clean_plotly_legend(),\r\n 35\t plotly::ggplotly(plots$river_mile_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 550) |> clean_plotly_legend()\r\n 36\t )\r\n 37\t} else {\r\n 38\t print(plots$tributary_plot)\r\n 39\t print(plots$river_mile_plot)\r\n 40\t}\r\n 41\t\r\n 42\t# source table download function\r\n 43\tsource(\"functions/table_download.R\")\r\n 44\tdownload_tbl(char = characteristic)\r\n 45\t\r\n 46\t# source threshold table function\r\n 47\tsource(\"functions/threshold_table.R\")\r\n 48\tshow_threshold_table(characteristic)\r\n 49\t\r\n 50\t# need to distinguish between total vs. field filtered (eg dissolved); (specified in ResultSampleFractionText). maybe a seperate plot or table to compare values. \r\n 51\t\r\n 52\t```" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } }, { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01NUevZozZEvS3W8jQoUBsD5", "type": "web_search_result" } ] } }, { "type": "reasoning", "text": "Now I have good information to share. Let me check the ADEC 18 AAC 70 specifically for the iron standard. The search results show some ADEC documents but don't specifically mention iron at 1 mg/L in those.\n\nKey findings:\n1. **USEPA**: The 1 mg/L (1000 µg/L) chronic aquatic life criterion for iron is indeed a real standard — but it originates from USEPA's **1976** \"Red Book\" (Quality Criteria for Water), not a 2014 document. Multiple sources confirm this: \"The current USEPA chronic iron criterion of 1000 µg/L (total recoverable) for protection of aquatic life was adopted in 1976.\" The iron chapter text currently cites \"USEPA, 2014\" which needs to be checked.\n\n\n2. **ADEC**: I need to verify whether ADEC explicitly lists iron at 1 mg/L in 18 AAC 70 or if they're referencing the USEPA criterion instead. The existing narrative cites both \"ADEC, 2008\" and \"USEPA, 2014,\" so I should check what ADEC actually adopted.\n3. Iron doesn't appear on the USEPA's national recommended water quality criteria table as a priority pollutant, but the 1976 Red Book standard of 1000 µg/L has been widely adopted across states like Iowa and Oregon, so this is a legitimate criterion even if it's not in the current priority list.\n\nThe 1 mg/L value itself is correct and commonly used, but I'm concerned about whether the \"USEPA 2014\" citation is accurate—the criterion actually traces back to 1976, and I haven't found evidence of a 2014 EPA document specifically revising iron standards. Let me verify what ADEC actually specifies in their regulations. I need to be transparent about what I can actually verify versus what requires further checking—the ADEC 2008 and USEPA 2014 citations in the chapter might refer to specific documents rather than the regulatory codes themselves, so I should flag that the 1976 origin of the USEPA criterion doesn't necessarily contradict those citations without seeing the actual sources. the existing chapter text more carefully, this chapter was written for the 2016 report. The citations (ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 2014) were presumably verified by whoever wrote the 2016 report. The 1 mg/L value itself is widely cited and appears correct.\n\nMy recommendation should be:\n1. The 1 mg/L value is well-supported\n2. The USEPA citation should probably be USEPA 1976 (the original Red Book criterion) rather than 2014 — needs verification\n3. The ADEC 2008 reference is plausible — could be an Alaska-specific document or cite to 18 AAC 70 standards from around that time\n4. Before adding to all_reg_vals.csv, Ben should verify the specific ADEC citation\n\nSo the 1 mg/L threshold is solid and ready to add to the plot, but I should flag that the USEPA citation likely points to 1976 rather than 2014, and Ben needs to confirm whether ADEC's standard is explicitly stated in 18 AAC 70 or if it adopts the USEPA criterion by reference.", "providerOptions": { } } }, { "type": "text", "text": "Here's what the research shows. I'll separate the threshold value from the citation question, because there's a discrepancy worth flagging.\n\n**The 1 mg/L value itself is well-supported.** The USEPA chronic iron criterion of 1,000 µg/L (1.0 mg/L, total recoverable) for protection of aquatic life was adopted in 1976 and is largely based on field observations of an iron-polluted Colorado stream in which trout and other fishes were absent at iron concentrations above that level. Most states adopted this EPA-recommended criterion of 1 mg/L (total iron) as the chronic criterion.\n\n**However, the citation in `iron.qmd` needs a closer look.** The chapter currently cites \"USEPA, 2014\" — but the USEPA iron criterion dates to 1976, not 2014. In 1999, EPA announced its intent to revise aquatic life criteria for iron, but no revised national criterion was published. Iron is a non-priority pollutant and does **not** appear on EPA's current National Recommended Water Quality Criteria table. So \"USEPA 2014\" may be a citation error from the 2016 report — the correct USEPA source is likely the 1976 \"Red Book\" (Quality Criteria for Water, USEPA 1976).\n\n**For the ADEC side**, I was not able to directly confirm an explicit iron entry in 18 AAC 70 from the search results. The ADEC 2008 citation in the chapter is plausible (could refer to a guidance document rather than the regulation itself), but you'd want to verify that independently.\n\n**Bottom line before touching `all_reg_vals.csv`:**\n- The **1 mg/L threshold value** is correct and appropriate to add.\n- The **\"USEPA, 2014\" citation** in `iron.qmd` line 14 should probably be corrected to \"USEPA, 1976\" — but you should confirm what document \"2014\" was intended to reference.\n- The **ADEC 2008 citation** should be verified against your copy of that document or 18 AAC 70.\n\nDo you want to proceed with adding 1 mg/L to `all_reg_vals.csv` now (subject to the citation fix separately), or would you prefer to resolve the citations first?\n\nVerified 1 mg/L iron threshold; flagged \"USEPA 2014\" citation as likely error (criterion dates to 1976)\nVerified the 1 mg/L iron threshold for the Kenai River water quality report. Confirmed the value is scientifically sound and widely adopted, but flagged that the \"USEPA 2014\" citation in iron.qmd is likely an error—the criterion originates from the USEPA 1976 Red Book. The ADEC 2008 citation also needs verification before finalizing." } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 2, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 29540, "cache_read_input_tokens": 57018, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 2750, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 2304, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global", \n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\nExcellent! Now I found what I need. Looking at the reference entries from Appendix 2 of the 2016 report:\n\n1. **USEPA, 2014** = \"USEPA, (2014), National Recommended Water Quality Criteria: USEPA data available on the World Wide Web, accessed June 17, 2015 at http://water.epa.gov/scitech/swguidance/standards/criteria/current/index.cfm\"\n\n2. **ADEC, 2008** = \"ADEC, (2012), Water Quality Standards, 18 AAC 70, data accessed on June 17, 2015 at http://dec.alaska.gov/commish/regulations/pdfs/18%20AAC%2070.pdf\" (Note: there's also a 2008 version)\n \nNow I'm looking at Table 60 on Lead, Zinc, Calcium, and Iron Water Quality Standards to find the specific references for iron. I need to check whether iron appears in the EPA's 2014 national recommended water quality criteria list.\n\nFrom my search, I can see that the 1 mg/L iron standard actually originates from the EPA's 1976 \"Red Book\" and was later included in the 1986 \"Gold Book,\" though the 2016 report cites a 2014 EPA reference—but iron doesn't appear on the current EPA National Recommended Water Quality Criteria table since it's classified as a non-priority pollutant.\n
\n\nNow let me find the iron-specific reference entry in the standards appendix.\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\nI see that Table 60 seems to be mislabeled here - Table 60 actually shows Magnesium, Nitrate, Phosphorus, DRO, GRO, RRO, BTEX (which is what Table 61 says in the TOC). This is a formatting issue in the DOCX. Let me look at the area before position 252881 to find where the actual Iron standards table is.Let me search specifically for the iron entry in the standards tables.\n
\n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\nNow I have the full picture. The iron entry in the 2016 report's Appendix 2 contains:\n\n**Iron References:**\n1. **ADEC, 2008**: \"Alaska Water Quality Criteria Manual for Toxic and Other Deleterious Organic and Inorganic Substances\" (ADEC 2008)\n2. **USEPA, 2014**: \"National Recommended Water Quality Criteria\" - the USEPA website criteria table accessed June 17, 2015\n\nSo \"USEPA, 2014\" in the 2016 report specifically refers to EPA's **National Recommended Water Quality Criteria** table (which was the 2014 version at time of writing). This table is available online and includes iron as a non-priority pollutant with a CCC of 1 mg/L.\n\n\nFor the 2026 report, I need to find the most current versions of these references. The EPA's National Recommended Water Quality Criteria table was last updated in 2014 and remains the most recent for iron, though the page URL has since changed to the current EPA website structure. The page itself was updated in February 2026, but iron as a non-priority pollutant still appears in the older criteria table.\n\nLooking at the current EPA NRWQC page, iron is listed with a CCC of 1.0 mg/L for freshwater. Since the user wants the most current references, I should cite the current NRWQC table if iron still appears there, or fall back to the original EPA documents (1976 Red Book or 1986 Gold Book) if it's been removed from the priority pollutant list.\n\nFor ADEC, the 2008 Alaska Water Quality Criteria Manual for Toxic and Other Deleterious Organic and Inorganic Substances is still the relevant source for iron standards, even though the current ADEC 18 AAC 70 regulations were updated in August 2024. The 1 mg/L value and both citations are correct—I just need to clarify that \"ADEC 2008\" specifically refers to that criteria manual.\n\nFor the USEPA citation, the current NRWQC table is the right source to use since iron has been listed as a non-priority pollutant with a CCC of 1 mg/L since the EPA's original 1976/1986 recommendations, and most states including Alaska adopted this standard. The 2016 report already used the NRWQC table as the citation, so I should keep that approach but potentially update to the current version of the table.\n
\n\nI found exactly what I needed. Here's the full picture from the 2016 report's Appendix 2 (Table 60):\n\n**Iron standard entry:** \"1 mg/L for a non-priority pollutant, fresh water, and CCC\"\n\n**With two citations:**\n1. **ADEC, 2008** — specifically the *Alaska Water Quality Criteria Manual for Toxic and Other Deleterious Organic and Inorganic Substances* (a separate criteria manual, not 18 AAC 70 itself)\n2. **USEPA, 2014** — *National Recommended Water Quality Criteria* table, accessed from EPA's website at the time of writing\n\nSo both citations in `iron.qmd` are correct and traceable. Here's what \"most current and appropriate\" looks like for the 2026 report:\n\n| Citation | 2016 report used | Best current version |\n|---|---|---|\n| ADEC | 2008 criteria manual | Same document — there's no newer ADEC criteria manual for toxic substances; 18 AAC 70 (effective 2024) is the regulation but the 1 mg/L criterion value itself comes from the 2008 manual |\n| USEPA | 2014 NRWQC table | The NRWQC table is still maintained at EPA's website (updated Feb 2026). Iron remains listed at 1 mg/L CCC. The URL has changed; original source document remains USEPA 1976 |\n\n**My recommendation:** The citations are already appropriate as-is. For the 2026 report, you could update the USEPA citation to reflect the current NRWQC table URL (no longer at `water.epa.gov/scitech/...`, now at `epa.gov/wqc/national-recommended-water-quality-criteria-aquatic-life-criteria-table`). The ADEC 2008 criteria manual is still the right document.\n\n**Bottom line for `all_reg_vals.csv`:** The 1 mg/L value is fully supported. The citations are solid. EPA|Iron|iron', line)) cat(line, '\\n---\\n')\n}\n\" 2>/dev/null | head -100" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 1945, "cache_read_input_tokens": 137043, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 1945, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 438, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 1945, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null }, "positai": { "timestamp": 1774994851190, "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6", "modelName": "Claude Sonnet 4.6", "providerId": "positai", "usage": { "inputTokens": 1, "outputTokens": 438, "totalTokens": 139427, "cacheReadTokens": 137043, "cacheWriteTokens": 1945, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 1945, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01Qvasy3jFuWrpVFWgCAGQBA", "toolName": "bash", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[1] \"✅ .Rprofile is running correctly\"\r\nFound References at positions: 257552" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_01EnU2nyDgT62E7tMgpc7vM8", "toolName": "bash", "input": { "command": "cd \"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx\" && Rscript -e \"\npath <- 'other/documents/references/Kenai Watershed Forum Baseline Water Quality Assessment 2016.docx'\ntmp <- tempdir()\nunzip(path, files='word/document.xml', exdir=tmp)\nraw <- readLines(file.path(tmp, 'word/document.xml'), warn=FALSE)\nraw <- paste(raw, collapse=' ')\nclean <- gsub('<[^>]+>', '', raw)\nclean <- gsub('[[:space:]]+', ' ', clean)\n# There's only one position. the references would be\n# The doc is long - references at page 197 means roughly in first half\n# Find where 'USEPA' appears many times\npositions <- gregexpr('USEPA', clean)[[1]]\ncat('USEPA appears', length(positions), 'times\\n')\ncat('First 5 positions:', positions[1:5], '\\n')\ncat('Middle positions:', positions[round(length(positions)/2)], '\\n')\n\" 2>/dev/null" }, "command": "cd \"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx\" && Rscript -e \"\npath <- 'other/documents/references/Kenai Watershed Forum Baseline Water Quality Assessment 2016.docx'\ntmp <- tempdir()\nunzip(path, files='word/document.xml', exdir=tmp)\nraw <- readLines(file.path(tmp, 'word/document.xml'), warn=FALSE)\nraw <- paste(raw, collapse=' ')\nclean <- gsub('<[^>]+>', '', raw)\nclean <- gsub('[[:space:]]+', ' ', clean)\n# Get text around the USEPA positions in the 40000-50000 range (where references section likely is)\nsection <- substr(clean, 30000, 55000)\ncat(section)\n\" "toolName": "bash", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[1] \"✅ .Rprofile is running correctly\"\r\ntaken at Russian River330Table 129: Arsenic, Cadmium, Chromium, Copper, Lead, Zinc, and Calcium samples taken at Juneau Creek.331Table 130: Iron, Magnesium, Nitrate, Phosphorus, DRO, GRO, and RRO samples taken at Juneau Creek.332Table 131: BTEX, Fecal, pH, Conductivity, TSS, Turbidity, and Water Temperature samples taken at Juneau Creek.333Table 132: Data Quality Analysis for Metals in Spring.339Table 133: Data Quality Analysis for Nutrients in Spring.341Table 134: Data Quality Analysis for Hydrocarbons in Spring.343Table 135: Data Quality analysis for Additional Parameters in Spring.347Conversions:1 gram = 1000 micrograms°F = 9/5(°C) + 32Unit Abbreviations:mg/L = milligrams per literμg/L = micrograms per literCFU/100m = coliform forming units per 100 millilitersμS/cm = microsiemens per centimeter NTU= nephelometric turbidity unitAcronyms:USEPA (United States Environmental Protection Agency) ADEC (Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation) DRO (Diesel Range Organics)GRO (Gasoline Range Organics) RRO (Residual Range Organics)BTEX (Benzene, Toluene, Ethylbenzene, and Xylenes)AbstractThe Kenai Watershed Forum and several governmental agencies formed a cooperative partnership to collect and analyze water samples from 13 locations along the Kenai River mainstem and from eight of its tributaries every spring and summer from 2000 to 20014. Laboratory analysis was conducted on dissolved metals, total metals, nutrients, hydrocarbons, fecal coliform bacteria, and several other parameters. These results are herein compared to Alaska and federal water quality standards for freshwater aquatic life. Total metals had relatively few exceedances, excluding zinc levels in Slikok Creek and Soldotna Creek. Iron levels consistently exceeded the standard, especially in the Kenai River estuary and in the tributaries. Calcium and magnesium do not have applicable Alaska or federal standards; however, they were highest in the estuary and in Soldotna Creek. Nitrate concentrations decreased from Kenai Lake to the estuary while phosphorus increased. In the lower river, median hydrocarbon concentrations exceeded the Alaska standard during the summer. Total suspended solids and turbidity levels were highest in the estuary and in the Killey River. Water temperatures exceeded several standards in the summer, especially in the Moose River and other tributaries. Further study and any necessary restoration should be considered for locations with exceedances of zinc, hydrocarbons, iron, and water temperature.IntroductionThis report summarizes water quality data collected between summer 2000 and summer 2014 from 21 sampling locations in the Kenai River mainstem and its tributaries. Local, state, federal, and tribal government entities, as well as several local area non-­‐profits, formed a cooperative partnership so that sampling teams from various agencies were able to collect water samples twice per year, once in the spring and once in the summer, and this effort continues beyond the publication of this report. The locations of the sampling sites are identified with maps, GPS coordinates, and photographs. Trends in the data are highlighted, and the results are compared to the Alaska and federal water quality standards for freshwater aquatic life.The water quality data focuses on metals, nutrients, hydrocarbons, fecal coliform bacteria, and various field parameters. Arsenic, cadmium, chromium, copper, lead, and zinc are the dissolved metals that have been analyzed, and calcium, iron, and magnesium were reported as total metals. Additionally, the report focuses on the nutrients nitrate and phosphorus. Specifically, the hydrocarbons that were collected and analyzed include diesel range organics, gasoline range organics, residual range organics, benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, m,p-­‐xylene, and o-­‐xylene. Fecal coliform bacteria, pH, specific conductance, total suspended solids, turbidity, and water temperature are the remaining parameters that have been included in the analysis.The results are displayed in graphs with associated written analyses and tables. The graphs display the median and variance for each parameter at a specific location, and these graphs are separated depending on whether the data was collected in the spring or in the summer. In some cases, parameters were present in levels that could not be detected by current laboratory analysis, and when this occurred with over 80% of the samples, scatter plots have been displayed omitting the unknown low levels. A trend analysis was conducted for the following parameters: Lead, Zinc and BTEX; based on the nature of the data, a polynomial line was used because it is the best way to capture and represent the fluctuations in the data. No trend analysis was conducted at sampling stations that had less than four detection instances during the fourteen years.Complete data tables can be found in Appendix 1 and 4, which are organized by parameter and site location, respectively.Description of the Study AreaLocated in southcentral Alaska, the Kenai River is part of the Cook Inlet Basin and is intricately linked to the surrounding communities through sport and commercial fishing, tourism, recreation, and the propagation of fish and wildlife (see Figure 1). Five species of Pacific salmon flourish in the Kenai River Watershed, comprising 30% of the commercial Chinook harvest and 40% of the commercial sockeye harvest (Glass, 1999). Surface runoff, groundwater composition, natural minerals, aquatic plants and animals, and human activities can affect water quality in this area (Glass, 1999). Potential sources of pollution from humans include gasoline powered boat engines, agriculture, mining, street runoff, and perforated septic tanks.AcknowledgementsThis study could not have been completed without the assistance and cooperation of many state agencies including the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation (ADEC), the Alaska Department of Natural Resources, and the Alaska Department of Fish and Game. The Kenaitze Indian Tribe, Cook Inlet Aquaculture Association, the Nature Conservancy, Analytica Laboratories, Kenai Peninsula Trout Unlimited and Taurianen Engineering and Testing (previously known as Northern Testing Laboratories) also supported this project. Additional cooperation transpired with the United States Forest Service, the United States Fish and Wildlife Service through the Kenai National Wildlife Refuge, the Kenai Peninsula Borough, the City of Soldotna, and the City of Kenai. Finally, many landowners graciously allowed access to the Kenai River and its tributaries from their property.Thank you to all the staff at the Kenai Watershed Forum that made this project possible. Special thanks to Robert Ruffner, Branden Bornemann, Grant Humphreys and Shannon Dillard who where very involved in the collection of data and completion of this report.MethodsWater samples were collected at 13 locations along the Kenai River mainstem and from eight tributaries near their confluence points (see Figure 2). These locations were chosen by dozens of participants in order to accurately represent the Kenai River Watershed’s ambient water quality conditions (Ashton, 1998). Sampling occurred in the spring and the summer each year beginning in summer 2000.After a half-­‐day training session, staff from governmental and non-­‐governmental agencies dispersed to sampling locations in teams of two or more to collect samples. All samples were collected on the same day, and the timing of the sampling coincided with an outgoing tide, near low tide, to reduce the potential of collecting water from Cook Inlet. Typically, the individual collecting the sample waded into the water until the water level was around two feet deep, and the sample was collected while facing upstream. If the individual collected the sample using a boat, the samples were collected from the bow while the boat faced upstream. The bottles were placed approximately one foot below the surface to collect the water samples and then preserved for transportation to the laboratory. Beginning in spring 2002, two duplicate samples were collected for quality control. These procedures follow the protocols established in a Quality Assurance Project Plan that was originally approved by the ADEC in 2001 and later revised and approved by ADEC again in 2013.The software used for the analysis is “R”and Microsoft Excel. In instances where data was reported as not detected the half of the MDL or MRL was used to estimate the values and run the analysis. The MRL was used because during the early years of the project, the lab only provided this information and not the MDL.Maps of the Kenai River Watershed146183847130Figure 1: Location of Kenai River WatershedFigure 2: Kenai River Water Quality Sample SitesWater Quality Assessment of the Kenai River Watershed from July 2000-­‐201422Sampling Site Description and PhotographKenai River Mainstem SitesKenai River Mile 82This site is near the Kenai Lake Outlet and Kenai Lake Bridge and is located at 60.492007 N and -149.810844 W. Samples are typically collected downstream of the boat launch.1146047176854Figure 3: Kenai River Mile 82 (Kenai Lake Outlet) on 07/26/05.Kenai River Mile 70This site is near Jim’s Landing and is located at 60.481392 N and -150.115020 W. The sample is typically collected 40 feet downstream of the boat launch.1146047183942Figure 4: Kenai River Mile 70 (Jim's Landing) upstream on 07/22/14.Kenai River Mile 50This site is near the Skilak Lake Outflow and is located at 60.467517N and -150.507789 W.Samples are typically collected between the swan signs off of the south bank.1146048178015Figure 5: Kenai River Mile 50 (Skilak Lake Outflow) on 04/22/08.Kenai River Mile 43This site is upstream of Dow Island and is located at 60.489844 N and -­‐150.636905W. The samples are typically collected 100 feet upstream of the point of Dow Island.1146047179820Figure 6: Kenai River Mile 43 (upstream of Dow Island) on 07/22/14.Kenai River Mile 40This site is near Bings Landing and is located at 60.515441 N and -150.702069 W. Samples are typically collected in front of the boat launch near the center of the river.1146048178015Figure 7: Kenai River Mile 40 (Bings Landing) on 04/26/11.Kenai River Mile 31This site is near Morgan’s Landing and is located at 60.498284 N and -150.863121 W. Sampling typically occurs down the abandoned steep road behind the headquarters building.1146048179655Figure 8: Kenai River Mile 31 (Morgan's Landing) on 05/01/12.Kenai River Mile 23This site is near Swiftwater Park and is located at 60.480338 N and -151.030847 W. Samples are typically collected mid-channel in front of the ramp.Figure 9: Kenai River Mile 23 (Swiftwater Park) on [no date].Kenai River Mile 21This site is near the Soldotna Bridge and is located at 60.476634 N and -151.082099 W. Samples are typically collected 20 feet downstream of the bridge on the south bank.1146048177662Figure 10: Kenai River Mile 21 (Soldotna Bridge) on 07/26/05.Kenai River Mile 18This site is near Poacher’s Cove and is located at 60.502005 N and -151.106973 W. Samples are typically collected mid-channel just downstream of an island.1146048178015Figure 11: Kenai River Mile 18 (Poacher's Cove) on 07/17/07.Kenai River Mile 12.5This site is near the Pillars Boat Launch and is located at 60.533743 N and -151.099258W. Samples are typically collected toward the center of the river across from the dock.1146048180663Figure 12: Kenai River Mile 12.5 (Pillars Boat Launch) on 07/17/07.Kenai River Mile 10.1This site is upstream of Beaver Creek and is located at 60.539279 N and -151.142263 W. Samples are typically collected 200 yards upstream of the Beaver Creek and Kenai River confluence. During July 2000, April 2001, and July 2001, samples were collected downstream of the Kenai River and Beaver Creek confluence, and no samples were collected from this site in April 2002.1146048183088Figure 13: Kenai River Mile 10.1 (upstream of Beaver Creek on 07/17/07Kenai River Mile 6.5This site is near Cunningham Park and is located at 60.5408100 N and -151.182780 W. Sampling typically occurs straight out from the public-use boardwalk and can vary due to thetidal stage.1146047180755Figure 14: Kenai River Mile 6.5 (Cunningham Park) on 07/26/05.Kenai River Mile 1.5This site is near the City of Kenai Dock and is located at 60.543680 N and -151.222940 W.Samples are typically collected at the north end of the public fueling dock.1146048183029Figure 15: Kenai River Mile 1.5 (Kenai City Dock) on 05/02/13.Kenai River Tributary SitesJuneau CreekThis site is located at 60.481392 N and -150.115020 W. The sample is typically collected 40 feet downstream of the boat launch at Alaska Wildlands.Figure 16: Juneau Creek looking downstream on 05/07/13.Russian RiverThis site is a Kenai River tributary and is located at 60.484622 N and -149.993955 W. Samples are typically collected 90 feet upstream of the sanctuary sign.1146047181345Figure 17: Russian River looking upstream on 07/26/05.Killey RiverThis site is a Kenai River tributary and is located at 60.481518 N and -150.632498 W. Sampling typically occurs 100 yards upstream from the Kenai River confluence across from the fish table.Figure 18: Killey River on 04/29/14.Moose RiverThis site is a Kenai River tributary and is located at 60.536870 N and -150.754724 W. Sampling typically occurs upstream of the parking area.Figure 19: Moose River on 04/26/05.Funny RiverThis site is a Kenai River tributary and is located at 60.489963 N and -150.860982 W. Samples are typically collected 75 feet downstream of the bridge.Figure 20: Funny River on05/01/12.Soldotna CreekThis site is a Kenai River tributary and is located at 60.483364 N and -151.057656 W. Sampling typically occurs mid-channel.1146048178002Figure 21: Soldotna Creek on 07/17/07.Slikok CreekThis site is a Kenai River tributary and is located at 60.482318 N and -151.127053 W. Samples are typically collected in the mid-channel of Slikok Creek.1146047178015Figure 22: Slikok Creek on 05/07/13.Beaver CreekThis site is a Kenai River tributary and is located at 60.548029 N and -151.143240 W.1146048183548Figure 23: Beaver Creek on 04/28/09.No Name CreekThis site is a Kenai River tributary and is located at 60.550888 N and -151.268417 W. Samples are typically collected approximately 500 feet upstream of the confluence with the Kenai River, just upstream of the footbridge.1146047180011Figure 24: No Name Creek on 07/22/09.Parameters and GraphsInterpreting Box PlotsEach of the following graphs display the sampling results for a specific parameter, like arsenic. Within these graphs, a box and extending lines represent the results reported at each sampling site. A horizontal line within the box corresponds to the median of the data. The box contains 50% of the data and the vertical lines display the minimum and maximum values. Any data points that fall outside of the acceptable range are outliers and are portrayed as small circles.1146047177620Figure 25: Diagram of the components of a box plotArsenicNatural sources of arsenic in the Cook Inlet Basin include volcanic ash, glaciation, and mineral deposits. Only a minimal contribution of arsenic results from human activities like wood preservation (Glass and Frenzel, 2001). Arsenic is naturally present as a compound in rocks within the Kenai River Watershed, and as a dissolved metal, it can be acutely or chronically toxic to fish (Glass, 1999). The Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation (ADEC) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) have set the standard at 150 micrograms per liter (µg/L) for freshwater aquatic life chronically exposed to arsenic and 10 micrograms per liter (µg/L) for drinking water (Appendix 2) (USEPA, 2014; ADEC, 2008).None of the samples exceeded the Alaska or federal standard for freshwater aquatic life at any sampling location in spring or summer. The highest level detected in the mainstem was 46.5 µg/L at Mile 1.5 in May 2007, and arsenic was not detected on many occasions below the method detection limit (MDL) of 0.25 µg/L (Table 6). In the Kenai River mainstem, Mile 1.5 had the highest median level in the spring event and summer monitoring event, followed by Mile 6.5 in the spring event and Mile 23 during the summer monitoring event (figures 26 & 28). In the mainstem, higher arsenic levels occurred in the spring samples, while the tributaries levels were higher during the summer with more detected levels between the years 2007-2014 than any of the previous years. (Tables 6 & 32)The highest concentration on the mainstem occurred at Mile 1.5 where arsenic was detected on every sampling event after 2005, while arsenic was detected on all sampling dates at Beaver Creek, Soldotna Creek, and Moose River. The concentrations of arsenic ranged from a high of 12.8 µg/L in Soldotna Creek in summer 2014 to below the MDL of 0.25 µg/L in many locations. Of the tributaries, Soldotna Creek had the highest median level, followed by Moose River and then Beaver Creek in summer and Soldotna, Beaver and Moose in spring. No Name Creek had the fewest incidences of arsenic detection of all the tributaries. (Tables 6 & 32)When comparing the arsenic levels to the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation standards for drinking water, the main stem at Mile 1.5 is the only station that presents multiple exceedances. All exceedances took place during the spring sampling events. (Tables 6 & 32)Concentrations of arsenic are generally lower in surface streams than in groundwater, which is typically the source of drinking water (Glass and Frenzel, 2001). The USEPA set the criterion for arsenic in drinking water at 10 µg/L because arsenic has been linked to cancer, skin damage, and circulatory problems (USEPA, 2003). Although the levels of arsenic reported in this study do not exceed the national criterion for the health of an aquatic community in freshwater, groundwater may contain concentrations that are hazardous to human health, and all sources of drinking water should be tested for arsenic.ADEC & EPA Aquatic Life Standard for Arsenic●●●●●●●●●●●● ● ●●●●●●Arsenic(ug/L)50150Arsenic Spring 2001−2014, samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.ADEC & EPA Aquatic Life Standard for 2000 to 2014 compared to ADEC drinking water standards.Since 45% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.ADEC & EPA Aquatic Life Standard for Arsenic●●*n=3●●Arsenic(ug/L)123458150Arsenic Spring 2001−2014, Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley RiverRussian RiverJuneau CreekFigure 30: Arsenic sampled in Kenai River tributaries during spring 2001 to 2014.Since 25% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.ADEC Drinking Water Standard for Arsenic●●*n=3●●Arsenic(ug/L)12345810Arsenic Spring 2001−2014, Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley RiverRussian RiverJuneau CreekFigure 31: Arsenic sampled in Kenai River tributaries during spring 2001 to 2014 compared to ADEC drinking water standards.Since 25% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.ADEC & EPA Aquatic Life Standard for Arsenic●●●●●*n=4●●●●●●Arsenic(ug/L)24681012150Arsenic Summer 2000−2014, Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley RiverRussian RiverJuneau CreekFigure 32: Arsenic sampled in Kenai River tributaries during summer 2000 to 2014. Since 20% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.ADEC Drinking Water Standard for Arsenic●●●●●*n=4●●●●●●Arsenic(ug/L)24681012Arsenic Summer 2000−2014, Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley RiverRussian RiverJuneau CreekFigure 33: Arsenic sampled in Kenai River tributaries during summer 2000 to 2014 compared to ADEC drinking water standards.Since 20% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.CadmiumCadmium is a rare elemental metal that can occur naturally in freshwater at concentrations of less than 0.1 µg/L, but at slightly increased concentrations, it can be toxic to aquatic life (USEPA, 2001). Additional cadmium can enter the hydrologic cycle as a component of fertilizer, pesticide, pigment, and as a result of iron and steel production, coal combustion, and mining waste (USEPA, 2001). The ADEC and the USEPA have set the standard for cadmium at a range of 0.0650 µg/L to 0.64 µg/L, depending on hardness, for chronically exposed freshwater aquatic life (Appendix 2) (USEPA, 2014; ADEC, 2008).In the mainstem, concentrations of cadmium ranged from a high of 8 µg/L at Mile 21 in summer 2000 to the lowest levels that were below the MDL of 0.062 µg/L that occurred in multiple locations. In the Kenai River mainstem, cadmium samples at Mile 21 and Mile 82 exceeded the standard on one occasion each. (Table 7)The highest level of cadmium ever detected in the tributaries was 63 µg/L at Soldotna Creek in summer 2002, and the lowest was below the MDL of 0.062 µg/L. Cadmium was detected once above the standard in Soldotna Creek and Slikok Creek, and all other tributary samples did not exceed the standard. (Table 33)Since so few cadmium samples were detected, trends were difficult to determine and should be interpreted with caution. In spring, cadmium was only reported four times, in contrast to summer samples in which it was reported ten times. In April of 2001, 2002, 2003, 2004, 2005 and summer 2001 through 2005, the method reporting limits (MRL) were mainly higher than the standard, so it is unknown whether these samples exceeded the standard. (Tables 7 & 33)SiteDateHardness (mg/L)Standard ((µg/L)Result Value (µg/L)Mile 217/18/0029.450.118Mile 827/20/0435.910.121Soldotna Creek7/16/0279.510.2563Slikok Creek7/31/1262.130.210.501Table 1: Summary table of hardness-­‐dependent exceedances for cadmium.Spring Summer ExceedancesCadmium(ug/L)18Cadmium Spring and Summer 2000−2014, Kenai River00.501.56.510.112.5182123314043507082River MileFigure 34: Cadmium sampled in the Kenai River mainstem during spring 2001 to 2014 and summer 2000 to 2014 with hardness-­‐dependent standards and exceedances.94% of the samples had results below the MRL or MDL, those results are not included in the graph.Spring Summer ExceedancesCadmium(ug/L)0.20.40.663Cadmium Spring and Summer 2000−2014, Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley RiverRussian RiverJuneau CreekFigure 35: Cadmium sampled in Kenai River tributaries during spring 2001 to 2014 and summer 2000 to 2014 with hardness-­‐dependent standards and exceedances.98% of the samples had results below the MRL or MDL, those results are not included in the graph.ChromiumIn rivers and streams, chromium is an elemental metal that typically exists as hexavalent or trivalent chromium (USEPA, 1980a). Non-natural sources of chromium salts include the metal finishing industry, textile manufacturing, leather tanning, paint, fungicides, and wood preservatives (USEPA, 1980a). At a concentration of 21 µg/L of hexavalent chromium, river algae cannot photosynthesize, and the growth in weight of Chinook salmon can be reduced by approximately ten percent" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "Now I found what I need! Looking at the text: \"The ADEC and the USEPA have set the standard for arsenic at 150 micrograms per liter (µg/L)... (USEPA, 2014; ADEC, 2008).\" The 2016 report consistently cites \"USEPA, 2014\" and \"ADEC, 2008\" for all water quality criteria. The USEPA 2014 reference in the 2016 report seems to be a generic citation for the current USEPA water quality criteria - probably referring to the National Recommended Water Quality Criteria page/document from 2014.\n\n\nLet me check the references section to find the full citation for \"USEPA, 2014.\"", "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "signature": "Ev4FClsIDBgCKkCInySfqrWGrB07YHPxTtj6OrKIntO09CJcJngwrN/94fSJvIHZqFNdk9dLGEFIiaJOwj3PD8QFQU9RFtYj3A4NMhFjbGF1ZGUtc29ubmV0LTQtNjgAEgz33ne8HoK4BU+6JMsaDH93WhzFR8KZOKJiYyIwaqlzygCLZBeU56vwmKZWHLZM3hPF4R/U2gRX8M7aW4hz+WKHb4nP9JckdMp+lyL9KtAEM8JbEXfuYpLbrXBFxwKROOX7uQKoUuorpCh75HQo5+bLi776Ylo2xim3yntZzPoKnzuCDEYzogW6pu4LqUzusfwNNY6A3Oj84hJzVsY60Cu6xqacjDXBqX8B6rOueZoBezyBGLp2r/LlCQ0otO8UvDpb/+fXVzVPAEGar9qorFUqtE0bccJOPns8MljAKbzgyGfSN97qo0bY22OTxN13gN/H146SCExa5Y0hz9/gvDGHY1X6LOfJDohgNllRK14kvOC8Y+bDL6skkyYp6HnSBAs0kRV1PmrT5+GDRoxoqy8aDLgaAKkicDzvXPR2c1u0VMe7WESIYxFZiY1TZ4iEj22//+ejveFo3su/OnMhF8+5WT9vXQCDOjV1W5DqsEAqAZ3bGn0qF0YjWyGtzcYgs1FOLW1q8+WWrKbPENBZQ+X6KWqvw90ldUQ9aFdwwQ8Js+ej0N4A8ApJQov4feWD/rmbcnIHYygQFy5cSevh3VICYKPx+dlvRUR7qgRncaYbsNylt6RSvMOL4weQcsIcbAbGJyj6+CCUCc51vU+5Snfov3vnGyMfTZ8Ui7o/A2njssPtKmgDW+mfiUnesCoJLddupfd9MIcr9dVgxfpBvRnW+ykJMvS4WtBt4nG/egmU8iN9LlOqfxtLDwOZLOzemXG2k75I7W6Um/JriCTMkqs2CgpIVou2Pprcw/Rz8A2FOotkzok91wy2pCrYgAvEetKl7OyJx2O/viNTPq3AQjovG2jWSJk2WIdgwoEEqBl/F7+J0/Yak2tM9rUn2/x2RBgB" } } }, { "type": "text", "text": "Now I can see the pattern. Let me get the actual reference list entries." }, { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_01GGDNDPMJyaJ7G4tfvCbGuR", "toolName": "bash", "input": { "command": "cd \"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx\" && Rscript -e \"\npath <- 'other/documents/references/Kenai Watershed Forum Baseline Water Quality Assessment 2016.docx'\ntmp <- tempdir()\nunzip(path, files='word/document.xml', exdir=tmp)\nraw <- readLines(file.path(tmp, 'word/document.xml'), warn=FALSE)\nraw <- paste(raw, collapse=' ')\nclean <- gsub('<[^>]+>', '', raw)\nclean <- gsub('[[:space:]]+', ' ', clean)\n# Find 'Literature Cited' section\npos <- regexpr('Literature Cited|LITERATURE CITED|Works Cited|REFERENCES', clean)[[1]]\nif(pos > 0) {\n cat('Found at position:', pos, '\\n')\n cat(substr(clean, pos, pos + 8000))\n} else {\n cat('Not found\\n')\n # try finding USEPA in a dense cluster\n positions <- gregexpr('USEPA', clean)[[1]]\n # look at the cluster around positions 45000-50000\n cat(substr(clean, 45000, 50000))\n}\n\" 2>/dev/null | head -150" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 7372, "cache_read_input_tokens": 146468, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 7372, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 558, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 7372, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null }, "positai": { "timestamp": 1774994895953, "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6", "modelName": "Claude Sonnet 4.6", "providerId": "positai", "usage": { "inputTokens": 1, "outputTokens": 558, "totalTokens": 154399, "cacheReadTokens": 146468, "cacheWriteTokens": 7372, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 7372, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01GGDNDPMJyaJ7G4tfvCbGuR", "toolName": "bash", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[1] \"✅ .Rprofile is running correctly\"\r\nNot found\r\ne portrayed as small circles.1146047177620Figure 25: Diagram of the components of a box plotArsenicNatural sources of arsenic in the Cook Inlet Basin include volcanic ash, glaciation, and mineral deposits. Only a minimal contribution of arsenic results from human activities like wood preservation (Glass and Frenzel, 2001). Arsenic is naturally present as a compound in rocks within the Kenai River Watershed, and as a dissolved metal, it can be acutely or chronically toxic to fish (Glass, 1999). The Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation (ADEC) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) have set the standard at 150 micrograms per liter (µg/L) for freshwater aquatic life chronically exposed to arsenic and 10 micrograms per liter (µg/L) for drinking water (Appendix 2) (USEPA, 2014; ADEC, 2008).None of the samples exceeded the Alaska or federal standard for freshwater aquatic life at any sampling location in spring or summer. The highest level detected in the mainstem was 46.5 µg/L at Mile 1.5 in May 2007, and arsenic was not detected on many occasions below the method detection limit (MDL) of 0.25 µg/L (Table 6). In the Kenai River mainstem, Mile 1.5 had the highest median level in the spring event and summer monitoring event, followed by Mile 6.5 in the spring event and Mile 23 during the summer monitoring event (figures 26 & 28). In the mainstem, higher arsenic levels occurred in the spring samples, while the tributaries levels were higher during the summer with more detected levels between the years 2007-2014 than any of the previous years. (Tables 6 & 32)The highest concentration on the mainstem occurred at Mile 1.5 where arsenic was detected on every sampling event after 2005, while arsenic was detected on all sampling dates at Beaver Creek, Soldotna Creek, and Moose River. The concentrations of arsenic ranged from a high of 12.8 µg/L in Soldotna Creek in summer 2014 to below the MDL of 0.25 µg/L in many locations. Of the tributaries, Soldotna Creek had the highest median level, followed by Moose River and then Beaver Creek in summer and Soldotna, Beaver and Moose in spring. No Name Creek had the fewest incidences of arsenic detection of all the tributaries. (Tables 6 & 32)When comparing the arsenic levels to the Alaska Department of Environmental Conservation standards for drinking water, the main stem at Mile 1.5 is the only station that presents multiple exceedances. All exceedances took place during the spring sampling events. (Tables 6 & 32)Concentrations of arsenic are generally lower in surface streams than in groundwater, which is typically the source of drinking water (Glass and Frenzel, 2001). The USEPA set the criterion for arsenic in drinking water at 10 µg/L because arsenic has been linked to cancer, skin damage, and circulatory problems (USEPA, 2003). Although the levels of arsenic reported in this study do not exceed the national criterion for the health of an aquatic community in freshwater, groundwater may contain concentrations that are hazardous to human health, and all sources of drinking water should be tested for arsenic.ADEC & EPA Aquatic Life Standard for Arsenic●●●●●●●●●●●● ● ●●●●●●Arsenic(ug/L)50150Arsenic Spring 2001−2014, Kenai River051015201.56.510.112.5182123314043507082River MileFigure 26: Arsenic Sampled at the Kenai River mainstem during spring 2001 to 2014.Since 36% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.●ADEC Drinking Water Standard for Arsenic●●●●●●●●●●● ● ●●●●●●Arsenic(ug/L)50Arsenic Spring 2001−2014, Kenai River051015201.56.510.112.5182123314043507082River MileFigure 27: Arsenic sampled in the Kenai River mainstem during spring 2001 to 2014 compared to ADEC drinking water standards.Since 36% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.ADEC & EPA Aquatic Life Standard for Arsenic●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●Arsenic(ug/L)4150Arsenic Summer 2000−2014, Kenai River00.51.521.56.510.112.5182123314043507082River MileFigure 28: Arsenic sampled in the Kenai River mainstem during summer 2000 to 2014.Since 45% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.ADEC Drinking Water Standard for Arsenic●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●●Arsenic(ug/L)410Arsenic Summer 2000−2014, Kenai River00.51.521.56.510.112.5182123314043507082River MileFigure 29: Arsenic sampled in the Kenai River mainstem during summer 2000 to 2014 compared to ADEC drinking water standards.Since 45% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.ADEC & EPA Aquatic Life Standard for Arsenic●●*n=3●●Arsenic(ug/L)123458150Arsenic Spring 2001−2014, Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley references would be there\n# The TOC says references are at page 197 - let me look at the dense USEPA cluster\npositions <- gregexpr('USEPA', clean)[[1]]\n# Find where references bunch up - look at 2nd/3rd USEPA pos\nfor(p in positions[2:5]) {\n cat('=== Position', p, '===\\n')\n cat(substr(clean, p-200, p+500), '\\n\\n')\n}\n\" 2>/dev/null" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 2104, "cache_read_input_tokens": 153840, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 2104, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 473, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 2104, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null }, "positai": { "timestamp": 1774994911727, "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6", "modelName": "Claude Sonnet 4.6", "providerId": "positai", "usage": { "inputTokens": 1, "outputTokens": 473, "totalTokens": 156418, "cacheReadTokens": 153840, "cacheWriteTokens": 2104, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 2104, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01L5Qd7zGRyunT4woYUuUcnP", "toolName": "bash", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[1] \"✅ .Rprofile is running correctly\"\r\n=== Position 45613 ===\r\nd as a dissolved metal, it can be acutely or chronically toxic to fish (Glass, 1999). The highest level detected in the mainstem was 46.5 µg/L at Mile 1.5 in May 2007, and arsenic was not detected on many occasions below the method detec \r\n\r\n=== Position 45804 ===\r\n Agency (USEPA) have set the standard at 150 micrograms per liter (µg/L) for freshwater aquatic life chronically exposed to arsenic and 10 micrograms per liter (µg/L) for drinking water (Appendix 2) (USEPA, 2014; ADEC, 2008).None of the samples exceeded the Alaska or federal standard for freshwater aquatic life at any sampling location in spring or summer. The highest level detected in the mainstem was 46.5 µg/L at Mile 1.5 in May 2007, and arsenic was not detected on many occasions below the method detection limit (MDL) of 0.25 µg/L (Table 6). In the Kenai River mainstem, Mile 1.5 had the highest median level in the spring event and summer monitoring event, followed by Mile 6.5 in the spring \r\n\r\n=== Position 47700 ===\r\nampling events. (Tables 6 & 32)Concentrations of arsenic are generally lower in surface streams than in groundwater, which is typically the source of drinking water (Glass and Frenzel, 2001). The USEPA set the criterion for arsenic in drinking water at 10 µg/L because arsenic has been linked to cancer, skin damage, and circulatory problems (USEPA, 2003). Although the levels of arsenic reported in this study do not exceed the national criterion for the health of an aquatic community in freshwater, groundwater may contain concentrations that are hazardous to human health, and all sources of drinking water should be tested for arsenic.ADEC & EPA Aquatic Life Standard for Arsenic●●●●●●●●●●●● ● ●●●●●●Arsenic(ug/L)50150Arsenic Spring 2001−2014, Kenai River051015201.56.510.112.5182123314043507082River MileFigure 26: Arsenic Sampled at" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_01NuhQ6jwTAV9UNmmj6FR67f", "toolName": "bash", "input": { "command": "cd \"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx\" && Rscript -e \"\npath <- 'other/documents/references/Kenai Watershed Forum Baseline Water Quality Assessment 2016.docx'\ntmp <- tempdir()\nunzip(path, files='word/document.xml', exdir=tmp)\nraw <- readLines(file.path(tmp, 'word/document.xml'), warn=FALSE)\nraw <- paste(raw, collapse=' ')\nclean <- gsub('<[^>]+>', '', raw)\nclean <- gsub('[[:space:]]+', ' ', clean)\n# Look at pos 102983 (middle cluster - probably reference list)\ncat(substr(clean, 102000, 108000))\n\" 2>/dev/null | head -150" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 0, "cache_read_input_tokens": 157293, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 0, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 234, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 0, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null }, "positai": { "timestamp": 1774994924174, "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6", "modelName": "Claude Sonnet 4.6", "providerId": "positai", "usage": { "inputTokens": 1, "outputTokens": 234, "totalTokens": 157528, "cacheReadTokens": 157293, "cacheWriteTokens": 0, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 0, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01NuhQ6jwTAV9UNmmj6FR67f", "toolName": "bash", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[1] \"✅ .Rprofile is running correctly\"\r\nng 2001−2014, Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley RiverRussian RiverJuneau CreekFigure 106: Phosphorus sampled in Kenai River during spring 2001 to 2014.Since 33% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.●●●●●●*n=4●●●●●Phosphorus (mg/L)0.20.40.61.3Phosphorus Summer 2000−2014, Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley RiverRussian RiverJuneau CreekFigure 107: Phosphorus sampled in Kenai River tributaries during summer 2000 to 2014. Since 44% of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL, these values have been estimated using values that are half of the MRL or MDL.Diesel Range OrganicsDiesel range organics (DRO) consist of diesel fuels and associated byproducts, and include the n-alkane range from C10 to C25 (ADEC, 2007). Although the ADEC and the USEPA do not have a standard for the chronic exposure of freshwater aquatic life to DRO, it was included in this study to cover a broad range of hydrocarbons and narrow down potential sources.In the mainstem, DRO concentrations ranged from a high of 0.29 mg/L that occurred at Mile 1.5 and Mile 31 during spring 2001 to the lowest levels that occurred at multiple locations at concentrations below the MDL of 0.0060 mg/L (Table 17). In the spring, DRO was reported only in 2001 at Mile 1.5, Mile 10.1, and Mile 31, and the reported levels were only slightly higher than the MRLs. The summer samples in the mainstem were all below the MDL or MRL, and therefore, more DRO was detected in the spring than in the summer (Figure 108).The highest level of diesel range organics was 0.37 mg/L at Soldotna Creek in summer 2000, and the lowest levels occurred in multiple instances below the MDL of 0.0060 mg/L (Table 43). In the spring, DRO was reported only in 2001 at Slikok Creek, Soldotna Creek, and Moose River, while during the summer, the only tributary to report DRO was Soldotna Creek in 2000. In the tributaries, there were also more instances of detection in the spring than in the summer. The concentrations observed in the tributaries were higher than those detected in the mainstem (Figure 109).Spring Summer ExceedancesDiesel Range Organics(mg/L)0.20.3Diesel Range Organics Spring 2001−2005 and Summer 2000−2007 Kenai River00.101.56.510.112.5182123314043507082River MileFigure 108: Diesel Range Organics sampled in the Kenai River mainstem during spring 2001 to 2005 and summer 2000 to 200799% of the samples had results below the MRL or MDL, so those results are not included in the graph.Spring Summer ExceedancesDiesel Range Organics(mg/L)0.10.20.30.40.5Diesel Range Organics Spring 2001−2005 and Summer 2000−2007 Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley RiverRussian RiverJuneau CreekFigure 109: Diesel Range Organics sampled in Kenai River tributaries during spring 2001 to 2005 and summer 2000 to 2007.96% of the samples had results below the MRL or MDL, so those results are not included in the graph.Gasoline Range OrganicsGasoline Range Organics (GRO) are comprised of gasoline fuels and by-products, including benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene (BTEX), and GRO corresponds to the nalkane range of C6 to C10 (ADEC, 2007). Although there is an ADEC standard specifically for BTEX, there is not an ADEC or USEPA standard applicable to GRO as a whole for the chronic exposure of freshwater aquatic life.The highest level of gasoline range organics was 38.3 µg/L at Mile 1.5 in summer 2002, and many samples were below the MDL of 3.0 µg/L. Gasoline range organics have only been detected in the summer at Mile 1.5, Mile 6.5, and Mile 10.1 in the mainstem and were never detected in the tributaries (Tables 18 & 44). Although no concentrations were reported in summer 2005, this may be due to the exceptionally high MRL of 100 µg/L. More gasoline range organics were detected in the summer since none were reported in the spring (Figures 110 & 111).Spring Summer ExceedancesGasoline Range Organics(ug/L)2025303540Gasoline Range Organics Spring 2001−2004 and Summer 2000−2005 Kenai River05101501.56.510.112.5182123314043507082River MileFigure 110: Gasoline Range Organics sampled in the Kenai River mainstem during spring 2001 to 2004 and summer 2000 to 2005.96% of the samples had results below the MRL or MDL, so those results are not included in the graph.NOT DETECTEDSpring Summer ExceedancesGasoline Range Organics(ug/L)510152025303540Gasoline Range Organics Spring 2000−2004 and Summer 2000−2005 Kenai River Tributaries0No Name CreekBeaver CreekSlikok CreekSoldotna CreekFunny RiverMoose RiverKilley RiverRussian RiverJuneau CreekFigure 111: Gasoline Range Organics sampled in Kenai River tributaries during spring 2001 to 2004 and summer 2000 to 2005.All of the samples are reported as below the MRL or MDL.Residual Range OrganicsHeavy fuel products, like asphalt or Bunker C fuel, are referred to as residual range organics (RRO) and include the n-alkane range of C25 to C36 (ADEC, 2007). RRO was included in this study to cover a broad range of hydrocarbons, however, there is no ADEC or USEPA standard for freshwater aquatic life chronically exposed to RRO.The only instance of detection in the mainstem occurred at Mile 23 with a concentration of 4.75 mg/L during summer 2000, and the lowest levels were reported in numerous locations below the MDL of 0.032 mg/L in the spring and the summer (Table 19). The highest concentration of RRO ever detected was 5.67 mg/L in Soldotna Creek during the summer of 2000, and the lowest levels in the tributaries occurred in multiple instances below the MDL of 0.032 mg/L (Table 45). In the spring, No Name Creek was the only location that reportedly contained RRO, and in summer 2004, No Name Creek also contained RRO. The only other tributary to report RRO was Soldotna Creek in" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "I'm not finding the references section through this approach. Let me take a different approach - look specifically at a specific character position in the document that might contain the reference list.\n\nActually, looking at the TOC, it says \"References197\" - meaning references start at page 197. The document is very long with lots of tables and data. Let me look at specific positions - the positions list showed USEPA appears at 102983 which gave me data tables. \n\nLet me instead look near the end of the narrative text section (before all the data tables) to find the references.\n\n\nLooking at the USEPA position clusters, the earlier ones around 45613 and 47700 are scattered through the narrative section, while the later ones near 102983 fall within the massive tables. "bash", "input": { "command": "cd \"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx\" && Rscript -e \"\npath <- 'other/documents/references/Kenai Watershed Forum Baseline Water Quality Assessment 2016.docx'\ntmp <- tempdir()\nunzip(path, files='word/document.xml', exdir=tmp)\nraw <- readLines(file.path(tmp, 'word/document.xml'), warn=FALSE)\nraw <- paste(raw, collapse=' ')\nclean <- gsub('<[^>]+>', '', readLines(file.path(tmp, 'word/document.xml'), warn=FALSE)\nraw <- paste(raw, collapse=' ')\nclean <- gsub('<[^>]+>', '', raw)\nclean <- gsub('[[:space:]]+', ' ', clean)\n# Search for 'Table 60' in positions: 23042 252881 \r\nTable 60: Lead, Zinc, Calcium, and Iron Water Quality Standards.ParameterADEC StandardUSEPA StandardReferencesMagnesiumNone applicable to aquatic life, fresh water, and applicable to aquatic life, fresh water, and chronic exposure.None applicable to a priority or non-priority pollutant, fresh water, and CCC.ResidualRange OrganicsNone applicable to aquatic life, fresh water, and chronic exposure.None applicable to a priority or non-priority pollutant, fresh water, and CCC.BTEXFor fresh water growth and propagation of fish, shellfish, other aquatic life, and wildlife: total aromatic hydrocarbons (TAH) in the water column may not exceed10 µg/L. There may be no concentrations of petroleum hydrocarbons, animal fats, or vegetable oils in shoreline or bottom sediments that cause deleterious effects to aquatic life. Surface waters and adjoining shorelines must be virtually freefrom floating oil, film, sheen, or discoloration.No comparable standard for a priority or non- priority pollutant, fresh water, and CCC.ADEC, (2012), Water Quality Standards, 18 AAC 70, data accessed on June 17, 2015 at http://dec.alaska.gov/commish/regulations/pdfs/18%20A AC%2070.pdfTable 61: Magnesium, Nitrate, Phosphorus, Diesel Range Organics, Gasoline Range Organics, Residual Range Organics, and BTEX Water Quality Standards.ParameterADEC StandardUSEPA StandardReferencesFecal Coliform BacteriaNot applicable to the sampling methods used in this study.Not applicable to the sampling methods used in this study.ADEC, (2012), Water Quality Standards, 18 AAC 70, data accessed on June 17, 2015 at http://dec.alaska.gov/commish/regulations/pdfs/18%20A AC%2070.pdfUSEPA, (1989), 1989 Total Coliform Rule Requirements, accessed June 17, 2015 at http://water.epa.gov/lawsregs/rulesregs/sdwa/tcr/regulation.cfmpHFor fresh water growth and propagation of fish, shellfish, other aquatic life, and wildlife: May not be less than 6.5 or greater than8.5. May not vary more than 0.5 pH units from natural conditions6.5-9 for a non-priority pollutant, fresh water, and CCC.ADEC, (2012), Water Quality Standards, 18 AAC 70, data accessed on June 17, 2015 at http://dec.alaska.gov/commish/regulations/pdfs/18%20A AC%2070.pdfUSEPA, (2014), National Recommended Water Quality Criteria: USEPA data available on the World Wide Web, accessed June 17, 2015 at http://water.epa.gov/scitech/swguidance/standards/criteria/current/index.cfmSpecific ConductanceNone applicable to the aquatic life, fresh water, and chronic exposure.None applicable to a priority or non-priority pollutant, fresh water, andCCC.Table 62: Fecal Coliform Bacteria, pH, and Specific Conductance Water Quality Standards.ParameterADEC StandardUSEPA StandardReferencesTotal Suspended SolidsFor fresh water growth and propagation of fish, shellfish, other aquatic life, and wildlife: The percent accumulation of fine sediment in the range of 0.1 mm to 4.0 mm in the gravel bed of waters used by anadromous or resident fish for spawning may not be increased by more than 5% by weight above natural conditions. In no case may the 0.1 mm to 4.0 mm fine sediment range in those gravel beds exceed a maximum of 30% by weight. In all other surface waters no sediment loads (suspended or deposited) that can cause adverse effects on aquatic animal or plant life, their reproduction or habitat maybe present.For fresh water fish and other aquatic life: Settleable and suspended solids" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "I see that Table 60 seems to be mislabeled here - Table 60 actually shows Magnesium, Nitrate, Phosphorus, DRO, GRO, RRO, BTEX (which is what Table 61 says in the TOC).
