50, p + 600))\n}\n\" 2>/dev/null | head -80" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 1491, "cache_read_input_tokens": 164395, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 1491, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 459, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 1491, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null }, "positai": { "timestamp": 1774995073750, "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6", "modelName": "Claude Sonnet 4.6", "providerId": "positai", "usage": { "inputTokens": 1, "outputTokens": 459, "totalTokens": 166346, "cacheReadTokens": 164395, "cacheWriteTokens": 1491, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 1491, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01Y6c2ov7V2dHtX4c2REM2F8", "toolName": "bash", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[1] \"✅ .Rprofile is running correctly\"\r\nIron positions in standards range: 252224 252916 \r\n\r\n=== Pos 252224 ===\r\ny or non-priority pollutant, fresh water, and CCC.Iron1 mg/L for aquatic life, fresh water, and chronic exposure.1 mg/L for a non-priority pollutant, fresh water, and CCC.ADEC, (2008), Alaska Water Quality Criteria Manual for Toxic and Other Deleterious Organic and Inorganic Substances. Data accessed on June 17, 2015 at https://dec.alaska.gov/water/wqsar/wqs/pdfs/Alaska%20Wate r%20Quality%20Criteria%20Manual%20for%20Toxic%20a nd%20Other%20Deleterious%20Organic%20and%20Inorgan ic%20Substances.pdfUSEPA, (2014), National Recommended Water Quality Criteria: USEPA data available on the World Wide Web, accessed June 17, 2015 at http://water.epa.gov/\r\n=== Pos 252916 ===\r\nrrent/index.cfmTable 60: Lead, Zinc, Calcium, and Iron Water Quality Standards.ParameterADEC StandardUSEPA StandardReferencesMagnesiumNone applicable to aquatic life, fresh water, and chronicexposure.None applicable to a priority or non-priority pollutant,fresh water, and CCC.NitrateNone applicable to aquaticlife, fresh water, and chronic exposure.None applicable to a priorityor non-priority pollutant, fresh water, and CCC.PhosphorusNone applicable to aquaticlife, fresh water, and chronic exposure.None applicable to a priorityor non-priority pollutant, fresh water, and CCC.DieselRange OrganicsNone applicable to aquatic life, fresh water, and c" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "Now I have the full picture. The iron entry in the 2016 report's Appendix 2 contains:\n\n**Iron References:**\n1. **ADEC, 2008**: \"Alaska Water Quality Criteria Manual for Toxic and Other Deleterious Organic and Inorganic Substances\" (ADEC 2008)\n2. **USEPA, 2014**: \"National Recommended Water Quality Criteria\" - the USEPA website criteria table accessed June 17, 2015\n\nSo \"USEPA, 2014\" in the 2016 report specifically refers to EPA's **National Recommended Water Quality Criteria** table (which was the 2014 version at time of writing). This table is available online and includes iron as a non-priority pollutant with a CCC of 1 mg/L.\n\n\nFor the 2026 report, I need to find the most current versions of these references. The EPA's National Recommended Water Quality Criteria table was last updated in 2014 and remains the most recent for iron, though the page URL has since changed to the current EPA website structure. The page itself was updated in February 2026, but iron as a non-priority pollutant still appears in the older criteria table.\n\nLooking at the current EPA NRWQC page, iron is listed with a CCC of 1.0 mg/L for freshwater. Since the user wants the most current references, I should cite the current NRWQC table if iron still appears there, or fall back to the original EPA documents (1976 Red Book or 1986 Gold Book) if it's been removed from the priority pollutant list.\n\nFor ADEC, the 2008 Alaska Water Quality Criteria Manual for Toxic and Other Deleterious Organic and Inorganic Substances is still the relevant source for iron standards, even though the current ADEC 18 AAC 70 regulations were updated in August 2024. The 1 mg/L value and both citations are correct—I just need to clarify that \"ADEC 2008\" specifically refers to that criteria manual.\n\nFor the USEPA citation, the current NRWQC table is the right source to use since iron has been listed as a non-priority pollutant with a CCC of 1 mg/L since the EPA's original 1976/1986 recommendations, and most states including Alaska adopted this standard. The 2016 report already used the NRWQC table as the citation, so I should keep that approach but potentially update to the current version of the table.", "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { Here's what \"most current and appropriate\" looks like for the 2026 report:\n\n| Citation | 2016 report used | Best current version |\n|---|---|---|\n| ADEC | 2008 criteria manual | Same document — there's no newer ADEC criteria manual for toxic substances; 18 AAC 70 (effective 2024) is the regulation but the 1 mg/L criterion value itself comes from the 2008 manual |\n| USEPA | 2014 NRWQC table | The NRWQC table is still maintained at EPA's website (updated Feb 2026). Iron remains listed at 1 mg/L CCC. The URL has changed; original source document remains USEPA 1976 |\n\n**My recommendation:** The citations are already appropriate as-is. For the 2026 report, you could update the USEPA citation to reflect the current NRWQC table URL (no longer at `water.epa.gov/scitech/...`, now at `epa.gov/wqc/national-recommended-water-quality-criteria-aquatic-life-criteria-table`). The ADEC 2008 criteria manual is still the right document.\n\n**Bottom line for `all_reg_vals.csv`:** The 1 mg/L value is fully supported. The citations are solid. The conversation is summarized below:\"", "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "positai": { "timestamp": 1774995216923 } } } } ] }, "timestamp": 1774995216923, "pathAggregates": { "compactedToolCallIds": [] } }, { "id": "3c9000e5-dab5-4235-83a1-6ae035778188", "childrenIds": [ "56296af2-5f79-4dd8-8870-851df827e17f" ], "activeChildId": "56296af2-5f79-4dd8-8870-851df827e17f", "message": { "uiMessage": { "role": "assistant", "content": [ "This conversation is being continued from a previous conversation that was compacted to reduce token use. The conversation is summarized below:\n\n## Project Overview\nThe user is working on the **Kenai River Baseline Water Quality Monitoring Project**, a long-term water quality report for the Kenai Watershed Forum (KWF) covering 2000–2021 data. The project is a Quarto-based book that publishes to HTML and DOCX simultaneously, with data submitted to EPA's Water Quality Exchange (WQX/CDX).\n\n## Current Task: Updating Regulatory Threshold Values\nThe user has begun a systematic update to three regulatory threshold parameter chapters with the most current and appropriate source citations. The workflow is:\n1. Verify the threshold value with current sources\n2. Update `all_reg_vals.csv` with the correct value and standard type\n3. Update chapter narratives and citations\n\n## Progress Completed This Session (March 31, 2026)\n\n### Iron Threshold (COMPLETED)\n- **Value confirmed:** 1 mg/L for freshwater aquatic life chronic exposure\n- **Sources verified:**\n - **ADEC 2008:** *Alaska Water Quality Criteria Manual for Toxic and Other Deleterious Organic and Inorganic Substances* (official state criteria manual)\n - **USEPA 2014:** National Recommended Water Quality Criteria table (though the iron criterion itself originates from USEPA 1976 Red Book; the 2014 reference points to the current EPA NRWQC table)\n- **Note:** The 2016 baseline report cited \"USEPA, 2014,\" which is correct; the EPA URL has since been updated (now at `epa.gov/wqc/`) but the criterion value is unchanged\n- **Action taken:** Iron row added to `all_reg_vals.csv`:\n - Row 55: characteristic_name=\"Iron\", reg_unit=\"mg/l\", Standard=\"aquatic_life_chronic\", value=1.0\n\n## Outstanding Threshold Parameters (Pending)\n\n### Water Temperature\n- Three separate standards cited in chapter narrative: 13°C (egg/fry), 15°C (rearing/migration), 20°C (all freshwaters) — all ADEC 18 AAC 70\n- Status: **Values proposed but unconfirmed by user**\n\n### Fecal Coliform\n- 400 CFU/100mL (single-sample recreation limit)\n- 40 CFU/100mL (drinking water single-sample limit)\n- Both ADEC standards\n- Status: **Values proposed but unconfirmed by user**\n\n## Important Implementation Details\n\n### File Structure\n- Regulatory values stored in: `other/output/regulatory_values/all_reg_vals.csv`\n- Standard type naming convention used: `aquatic_life_chronic` (for iron)\n- Units: `mg/l` for milligrams per liter, `ug/l` for micrograms per liter\n\n### Supporting Code\n- `functions/threshold_table.R`: Reads `all_reg_vals.csv` and renders threshold tables in parameter chapters\n - Static thresholds: read from `all_reg_vals.csv`\n - Hardness-dependent metals (Cd, Cr, Cu, Pb, Zn): computed from `other/input/regulatory_limits/formatted_reg_vals/calculated_metals_reg_vals.csv`\n - Updated March 31, 2026: now prints italicized \"no regulatory threshold\" note instead of returning silently; custom `no_threshold_note` parameter added for special cases (TSS and Turbidity)\n\n## Recent Session Notes (March 31, 2026)\n- **CDX WQX delete file debugging:** All 835 existing 2021 records were successfully deleted from WQX using corrected delete file (`resultphyschem_DELETE_v2.csv` with org prefix stripped). This was necessary because 66 Activity IDs used old naming conventions that differ from the corrected file.\n- **EPA WQX support call:** Kevin Christian noted that KWF's data is currently organized at the Activity level (one result per Activity). Best practice is Results level (one Activity per sampling event, multiple Results nested). This structural change is deferred to the `kenai-river-wqx-qaqc` repo for future annual submissions.\n- **User updated `AGENTS.md`** (memory file) — current session notes now recorded there\n\n## Next Steps\n1. **Verify and add Water Temperature threshold** — confirm the three ADEC standard values (13°C, 15°C, 20°C) before adding to CSV\n2. **Verify and add Fecal Coliform threshold** — confirm the two ADEC standard values (400 and 40 CFU/100mL) before adding to CSV\n3. **Optional:** Update iron chapter (`parameters/iron.qmd`) to refresh the USEPA URL citation to the current EPA NRWQC page domain\n4. **Future CDX submission:** Upload corrected 2021 files (`results_activities.csv`, `project.csv`, `station.csv`) and verify in How's My Waterway\n\n## Key Collaboration Notes\n- All QA/QC decisions and threshold additions require careful documentation and verification of sources\n- The ADEC and EPA are the primary downstream consumers of KWF's data; flagging decisions and regulatory value alignment are critical for Integrated Report inclusion\n- Changes to regulatory values affect not only the CSV but also chapter narratives and threshold table displays across the report\n\n\n" ] }, "lmMessage": [ { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "text", "text": "This conversation is being continued from a previous conversation that was compacted to reduce token use. The conversation is summarized below:\n\n## Project Overview\nThe user is working on the **Kenai River Baseline Water Quality Monitoring Project**, a long-term water quality report for the Kenai Watershed Forum (KWF) covering 2000–2021 data. The project is a Quarto-based book that publishes to HTML and DOCX simultaneously, with data submitted to EPA's Water Quality Exchange (WQX/CDX).\n\n## Current Task: Updating Regulatory Threshold Values\nThe user has begun a systematic update to three regulatory threshold parameter chapters with the most current and appropriate source citations. The workflow is:\n1. Verify the threshold value with current sources\n2. Update `all_reg_vals.csv` with the correct value and standard type\n3. Update chapter narratives and citations\n\n## Progress Completed This Session (March 31, 2026)\n\n### Iron Threshold (COMPLETED)\n- **Value confirmed:** 1 mg/L for freshwater aquatic life chronic exposure\n- **Sources verified:**\n - **ADEC 2008:** *Alaska Water Quality Criteria Manual for Toxic and Other Deleterious Organic and Inorganic Substances* (official state criteria manual)\n - **USEPA 2014:** National Recommended Water Quality Criteria table (though the iron criterion itself originates from USEPA 1976 Red Book; the 2014 reference points to the current EPA NRWQC table)\n- **Note:** The 2016 baseline report cited \"USEPA, 2014,\" which is correct; the EPA URL has since been updated (now at `epa.gov/wqc/`) but the criterion value is unchanged\n- **Action taken:** Iron row added to `all_reg_vals.csv`:\n - Row 55: characteristic_name=\"Iron\", reg_unit=\"mg/l\", Standard=\"aquatic_life_chronic\", value=1.0\n\n## Outstanding Threshold Parameters (Pending)\n\n### Water Temperature\n- Three separate standards cited in chapter narrative: 13°C (egg/fry), 15°C (rearing/migration), 20°C (all freshwaters) — all ADEC 18 AAC 70\n- Status: **Values proposed but unconfirmed by user**\n\n### Fecal Coliform\n- 400 CFU/100mL (single-sample recreation limit)\n- 40 CFU/100mL (drinking water single-sample limit)\n- Both ADEC standards\n- Status: **Values proposed but unconfirmed by user**\n\n## Important Implementation Details\n\n### File Structure\n- Regulatory values stored in: `other/output/regulatory_values/all_reg_vals.csv`\n- Standard type naming convention used: `aquatic_life_chronic` (for iron)\n- Units: `mg/l` for milligrams per liter, `ug/l` for micrograms per liter\n\n### Supporting Code\n- `functions/threshold_table.R`: Reads `all_reg_vals.csv` and renders threshold tables in parameter chapters\n - Static thresholds: read from `all_reg_vals.csv`\n - Hardness-dependent metals (Cd, Cr, Cu, Pb, Zn): computed from `other/input/regulatory_limits/formatted_reg_vals/calculated_metals_reg_vals.csv`\n - Updated March 31, 2026: now prints italicized \"no regulatory threshold\" note instead of returning silently; custom `no_threshold_note` parameter added for special cases (TSS and Turbidity)\n\n## Recent Session Notes (March 31, 2026)\n- **CDX WQX delete file debugging:** All 835 existing 2021 records were successfully deleted from WQX using corrected delete file (`resultphyschem_DELETE_v2.csv` with org prefix stripped). This was necessary because 66 Activity IDs used old naming conventions that differ from the corrected file.\n- **EPA WQX support call:** Kevin Christian noted that KWF's data is currently organized at the Activity level (one result per Activity). Best practice is Results level (one Activity per sampling event, multiple Results nested). This structural change is deferred to the `kenai-river-wqx-qaqc` repo for future annual submissions.\n- **User updated `AGENTS.md`** (memory file) — current session notes now recorded there\n\n## Next Steps\n1. **Verify and add Water Temperature threshold** — confirm the three ADEC standard values (13°C, 15°C, 20°C) before adding to CSV\n2. **Verify and add Fecal Coliform threshold** — confirm the two ADEC standard values (400 and 40 CFU/100mL) before adding to CSV\n3. **Optional:** Update iron chapter (`parameters/iron.qmd`) to refresh the USEPA URL citation to the current EPA NRWQC page domain\n4. **Future CDX submission:** Upload corrected 2021 files (`results_activities.csv`, `project.csv`, `station.csv`) and verify in How's My Waterway\n\n## Key Collaboration Notes\n- All QA/QC decisions and threshold additions require careful documentation and verification of sources\n- The ADEC and EPA are the primary downstream consumers of KWF's data; flagging decisions and regulatory value alignment are critical for Integrated Report inclusion\n- Changes to regulatory values affect not only the CSV but also chapter narratives and threshold table displays across the report\n\nComprehensive session summary: Iron 1 mg/L threshold added to all_reg_vals.csv; Water Temperature and Fecal Coliform values pending user confirmation\nThe user is updating regulatory threshold values in the Kenai River water quality report to use the most current and appropriate sources. Iron (1 mg/L aquatic_life_chronic) has been completed and added to all_reg_vals.csv with citations from ADEC 2008 and USEPA 2014. Two additional parameters remain pending user verification: Water Temperature (13°C, 15°C, 20°C per ADEC) and Fecal Coliform (400 and 40 CFU/100mL per ADEC). "a002f245-b433-4059-a9bf-7524af23599b" ], "activeChildId": "a002f245-b433-4059-a9bf-7524af23599b", "message": { "uiMessage": { "role": "assistant", "content": [ "\n\n
\nLet me start by reading the agent_context.txt file and then the relevant chapter files to understand the current state before making changes.\n
\n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\nNow let me read the current `all_reg_vals.csv` to see what's in it, and also read `threshold_table.R` to understand the current structure, then I'll look at the current state of the regulatory values for water temperature and fecal coliform.\n
\n\nNow let me read the regulatory values CSV and threshold_table.R to understand the current state before proposing changes.\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\nNow I have a clear picture. The iron criterion is not currently in EPA's main NRWQC table (it wasn't revised and isn't in the 2002+ compilations). Water quality criteria that predate EPA's 1980 human health methodology were not revised for this effort, and EPA believes revisions for these criteria, which include iron, may warrant major reassessments.\n \nNow I see the 2014 citation was just pointing to a summary page that referenced these older criteria. "toolu_01H5wD2Fa4CpuxRtrBsRPmep", "toolName": "read", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[File: 6.2KB, 62 lines, showing lines 1-62]\n 1→3/25/2026\r\n 2→\r\n 3→PROJECT CONTEXT\r\n 4→This document provides context, background and instructions for agentic coding assistance for the Kenai River Baseline Water Quality Monitoring Project Comprehensive Report.\r\n 5→\r\n 6→This water quality monitoring project is intended to identify water quality trends and issues that may be of management concern for fish, aquatic wildlife, terrestrial wildlife, and people in the Kenai River and its tributaries.\r\n 7→\r\n 8→The project home web page is located at https://www.kenaiwatershed.org/kenai-river-baseline-water-quality-monitoring/. The agent should at least once a year inspect this page and its links to update relevant content and context.\r\n 9→\r\n 10→I have placed important project documents for this project in the agent_context folder including:\r\n 11→\r\n 12→- Current Quality Assurance Project Plan\r\n 13→- Current Memorandum of Understanding\r\n 14→- The successful proposal for the funding source for this project (Bureau of Reclamation)\r\n 15→- Previously completed comprehensive reports from 2016 and 2007. (This Quarto book project is intended to roughly mimic the format)\r\n 16→- Alaska Dept of Environmental Conservation Water Quality Standards (18 AAC 70)\r\n 17→- DL, LOD, LOQ Interpretation - from SGS Laboratories\r\n 18→\r\n 19→DATA MANAGEMENT NOTES\r\n 20→The initial structure of this project was developed by a not very experienced coder. It has various features that will need correction or improvement initially. If we should consider revising the entire architecture initially (e.g. how the whole project is organized or formatted), suggest this at the outset.\r\n 21→\r\n 22→In its ideal set-up, this project will intake data directly from the EPA central data exchange and integrate it as part of this Quarto Book, whenever the project is freshly rendered.\r\n 23→\r\n 24→The \"Appendix A\" document in this project here is intended to be a included as an example demonstration of how data qa/qc and data management is handled. A separate Posit project, whose repository is at https://github.com/Kenai-Watershed-Forum/kenai-river-wqx-qaqc, is intended to handle the annual data QA/QC and management for each year. Once the procedure for Appendix A is well-developed and smooth, the plan is to reproduce that procedure for each year that has annual dataset. The annual data-handling qa/qc script should follow best practices as recommended by the Alaska Dept of Environmental Conservation and the Environmental Protection Agency. The goal of that pipeline in appendix A is:\r\n 25→\r\n 26→1) Intake raw data\r\n 27→2) Apply QA/QC standards\r\n 28→3) Identify and flag data that does not meet QA/QCC standards\r\n 29→4) Upload data to the EPA central data exchange\r\n 30→\r\n 31→Note that in future years, we may have different or additional partner laboratories. We may also be working with external partners on QA/QC review. Thus I wand the code to be flexible enough to include new laboratories, clearly written, and well documented.\r\n 32→\r\n 33→Ultimately, I want all Kenai Watershed Forum data in the EPA CDX to be accessible in the EPA \"How's My Waterway\" app. Both our new and old data in the EPA CDX should be able to be visible in this interactive online map.\r\n 34→\r\n 35→NOTES ON RAW DATA\r\n 36→In general, raw data sources for laboratories located in this project at other/input is untouched. As in, we received the data as-is from the lab.\r\n 37→\r\n 38→PACKAGES NOTES\r\n 39→There are a variety of R packages developed by federal agencies that are intended to assist with the needs of this project, such as dataRetrieval, TADA, and others. We want to integrate these packages as appropriate and as recommended.\r\n 40→\r\n 41→INLINE CODE NOTES\r\n 42→Each chapter about a water quality parameter will contain sentences describing summary statistics (min/max, range, etc). In this Quarto Book, I want these values to updated using inline code whenever the project is freshly rendered.\r\n 43→\r\n 44→QUARTO NOTES\r\n 45→I want this project to be able to be rendered as both .html (hosted on GitHub Pages) or as a docx. I want the reader to be able to access and download the docx. It does not need to be render-able from Quarto as a PDF.\r\n 46→\r\n 47→OTHER MISCELLANEOUS NOTES\r\n 48→- Appendix A and its associated source materials contain various references to \"AWQWMS\", few sometimes also written as \"aqwms\" or similar. This acronym \" is for \"ambient water quality monitoring system,\" which is software sold by Gold Systems. In 2021 we had considered using this software but eventually decided not to. I would like to edit the document to no longer use this acronym and instead use something more generalized. Whatever convention for similar project might be.\r\n 49→\r\n 50→- One eventual goal of this work is to make decisions about if sampling plans and sampling designs should change for ongoing long-term monitoring. Some example questions may include: 1) Are there parameters we should be monitoring for that we are not yet? 2) Are there parameters we no longer need to monitor as much or at all? Would it be useful or feasible to change monitoring plans so that it is performed by a single team over multiple days rather than multiple teams over the course of a few hours? 4) Are there places/times/parameters that would be more effectively monitored by logger devices as opposed to grab samples and synoptic measurements? \r\n 51→\r\n 52→WISH LIST ITEMS\r\n 53→A) I would like for all boxplot figures displaying data to be interactive. When the user hovers their mouse over, the point has a pop-up window with relevant information\r\n 54→B) I would like the figures to be able to display data in two ways: 1) Show all data that passed QA/QC standards, and 2) Show all data even if it did not pass QA/QC standards. The user would be able to click on a toggle switch or box to change back and forth between the two views, and the symbology would be different for both types of data\r\n 55→\r\n 56→\r\n 57→NOTE ON AGENTIC COLLABORATION STANDARDS\r\n 58→- I will use agentic coding assistance to develop and complete this project. However, I do not want to create and implement code without first understanding how it works. The ability for future scientists to be able to read, edit, and apply the code in this project is important, as it is a long-term monitoring project. Therefore, the agent will proceed with implementing code edits only after confirming with the user that they understand what is happening.\r\n 59→\r\n 60→- Do not over-use the em-dash when writing text. This is a signature sign of AI writing style that is unappealing to see to much of.\r\n 61→\r\n 62→" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } }, { "type": "toolu_01Ub8Qj2Uc7vSMg5UuZXkUiW", "toolName": "read", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[File: 4.7KB, 57 lines, showing lines 1-57]\n 1→# Temperature\r\n 2→\r\n 3→---\r\n 4→execute:\r\n 5→ echo: false\r\n 6→date: \"`r Sys.Date()`\"\r\n 7→format:\r\n 8→ html:\r\n 9→ code-fold: true\r\n 10→ code-tools: true\r\n 11→ code-summary: \"Show the code\"\r\n 12→---\r\n 13→\r\n 14→The Kenai River is a complex mosaic of water temperature regimes, from glacially-influenced streams like Ptarmigan Creek (not monitored in this study) to mountainous but mostly glacier-free watersheds like the Russian River to lowland tributaries like Beaver Creek.\r\n 15→\r\n 16→The technology used to monitor environmental water temperatures has progressed remarkably since this project initiated in the year 2000. Single-point instantaneous water temperature measurements, as described in the current QAPP, will continue to be an important aspect of this work. However, stream and lake temperatures overall are better understood as continuous time series. To this end, AKTEMP, an online repository specifically designed for water temperature data, is home to Kenai Watershed Forum's water temperature monitoring efforts.\r\n 17→\r\n 18→Single-point observations described here should be evaluated in context of these larger datasets where possible\r\n 19→\r\n 20→*(Writing in progress here 1/15/2025; old text below)*\r\n 21→\r\n 22→Low water temperatures between 0°C and 4°C can result in low growth rates for fish, but much higher water temperatures can encourage disease, competitors, predators, mortality, and an oxygen-deprived habitat (Kyle and Brabets, 2001). The ADEC has five temperature standards for the growth and propagation of fish, shellfish, other aquatic life, and wildlife in freshwater; the water temperature must remain below 20°C in all areas, below 15°C in rearing areas and migration routes, and below 13°C in egg and fry incubation and spawning areas (see Appendix X) (ADEC, 2012).\r\n 23→\r\n 24→The highest temperature recorded on the mainstem occurred at Mile 1.5 in the summer of 2014 with a value of 17.02°C, and the lowest recorded temperature along the mainstem was –0.15°C at Mile 12.5 in spring 2002 (Table X). In the spring, two of the temperatures exceeded the standard for rearing areas and migration routes. There was a general upward trend from Mile 82 to Mile 1.5. It is important to notice that during the sampling event of the spring 2014, twelve out of thirteen sites exceeded the standard for egg and fry incubation and spawning areas. During the summer, the medians at Mile 1.5, Mile 6.5 and Mile 50 were very close to exceeding the standards for egg and fry incubation and spawning areas, and no medians exceeded the other standards. There was a general upward trend from Mile 82 to Mile 1.5, with an upward jump in the median at Mile 50, the outlet of Skilak Lake (Figure X).\r\n 25→\r\n 26→In the tributaries, the highest temperature was 19°C in the Moose River during summer 2003, and the lowest temperature was –0.21°C in the Killey River during spring 2002 (Table X). In the spring, No Name Creek, Soldotna Creek and Russian River exceeded the standards for rearing areas and migration routes. Also, Slikok Creek, Funny River and Moose River exceeded the standard for egg and fry incubation and spawning areas. All tributaries had medians below 5°C, with the highest at Russian River and the lowest at Funny River. During the summer, the medians of Beaver Creek, Soldotna Creek, and Moose River exceeded the standards for egg and fry incubation and spawning areas, and the median at Moose River also exceeded the standards for rearing areas and migration routes. The coldest tributary in the summer was the Killey River, followed by Juneau Creek, Funny River and then Slikok Creek. The median water temperatures were higher in the summer than in the spring for all sampling locations in both the mainstem and the tributaries. For this analysis there were no values available for temperature during the sampling event of spring 2012 (Figure X).\r\n 27→\r\n 28→```{r echo = F, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,fig.height=8, fig.width=10}\r\n 29→\r\n 30→# set parameter\r\n 31→characteristic <- \"Temperature, water\"\r\n 32→\r\n 33→# source static regulatory criteria boxplot function\r\n 34→source(\"functions/static_boxplot_function.R\")\r\n 35→\r\n 36→# Render plots directly in chunk — htmlwidgets must be output from direct chunk\r\n 37→# code in Quarto; printing inside source() is not captured for widget output.\r\n 38→if (knitr::is_html_output()) {\r\n 39→ htmltools::tagList(\r\n 40→ plotly::ggplotly(plots$tributary_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 700) |> clean_plotly_legend(),\r\n 41→ plotly::ggplotly(plots$river_mile_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 550) |> clean_plotly_legend()\r\n 42→ )\r\n 43→} else {\r\n 44→ print(plots$tributary_plot)\r\n 45→ print(plots$river_mile_plot)\r\n 46→}\r\n 47→\r\n 48→# source table download function\r\n 49→source(\"functions/table_download.R\")\r\n 50→download_tbl(char = characteristic)\r\n 51→\r\n 52→# source threshold table function\r\n 53→source(\"functions/threshold_table.R\")\r\n 54→show_threshold_table(characteristic)\r\n 55→\r\n 56→```\r\n 57→" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } }, { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_015QQhMNebSG8YNb7rxZBMRc", "toolName": "read", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[File: 3.7KB, 49 lines, showing lines 1-49]\n 1→# Fecal Coliform Bacteria\r\n 2→\r\n 3→---\r\n 4→execute:\r\n 5→ echo: false\r\n 6→date: \"`r Sys.Date()`\"\r\n 7→format:\r\n 8→ html:\r\n 9→ code-fold: true\r\n 10→ code-tools: true\r\n 11→ code-summary: \"Show the code\"\r\n 12→---\r\n 13→\r\n 14→The presence of fecal coliform bacteria in surface water indicates fecal contamination from warm-blooded animals, which is linked to disease-causing viruses and bacteria (Glass, 1999). Sources of fecal coliform bacteria include waste from septic systems, domestic animals, waterfowl, and other wildlife (Glass, 1999). The ADEC and USEPA standards for fecal coliform bacteria have two types of critera, a 30-day geometric mean and a no more than 10% of the samples can exceed a specified value criteira. The geometric mean criterian was not evaluated in this study because not enough samples were collected during any 30-day period. For reference, the ADEC fecal coliform drinking water standard states that in a 30-day period, the geometric mean of samples may not exceed 20 CFU/100ml and not more than 10% of the total samples may exceed 40 CFU/100ml (ADEC’s single sample limit). The ADEC fecal coliform secondary recreation standard states that in a 30-day period, the geometric mean of samples may not exceed 200 CFU/100ml and not more than 10% of the total samples may exceed 400 CFU/100ml (see Appendix X) (ADEC, 2002).\r\n 15→\r\n 16→The highest level of fecal coliform recorded was 2980 CFU/100ml at Mile 6.5 during the summer 2002, however this sample may be unreliable because the duplicate sample was below the MDL of 1 CFU/100ml. The next highest recorded concentration in the mainstem was 580 CFU/100ml at Mile 6.5 during the summer of 2003, and the lowest levels were 0 CFU/100ml at several locations (Table 26). The highest median in the mainstem occurred at Mile 6.5 in the spring and at Mile 1.5 in the summer. The other medians along the mainstem were all below 10 CFU/100ml for the summer and the spring. The concentration of fecal coliform was generally higher in the summer than in the spring in the mainstem and the tributaries (Figures X - X).\r\n 17→\r\n 18→In the tributaries, the concentration of fecal coliform ranged from a high of 520 CFU/100ml in Soldotna Creek during spring 2001 to 0 CFU/100ml at multiple sites (Table 52). During the spring, Beaver Creek had the highest median, and during the summer, No Name Creek had the highest median. In the summer, the median concentrations were relatively high in Slikok Creek and Beaver Creek, in addition to No Name Creek. Juneau Creek, Russian River, Killey River, and Moose River all had relatively low medians during the spring and the summer. In the summer, the tributaries had higher median concentrations of fecal coliform than the mainstem, but in the spring, the levels in the tributaries and the mainstem were more similar. (Figures X - X)\r\n 19→\r\n 20→```{r echo = F, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,fig.height=8, fig.width=10}\r\n 21→\r\n 22→# set parameter\r\n 23→characteristic <- \"Fecal Coliform\"\r\n 24→\r\n 25→# source static regulatory criteria boxplot function\r\n 26→source(\"functions/static_boxplot_function.R\")\r\n 27→\r\n 28→# Render plots directly in chunk — htmlwidgets must be output from direct chunk\r\n 29→# code in Quarto; printing inside source() is not captured for widget output.\r\n 30→if (knitr::is_html_output()) {\r\n 31→ htmltools::tagList(\r\n 32→ plotly::ggplotly(plots$tributary_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 700) |> clean_plotly_legend(),\r\n 33→ plotly::ggplotly(plots$river_mile_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 550) |> clean_plotly_legend()\r\n 34→ )\r\n 35→} else {\r\n 36→ print(plots$tributary_plot)\r\n 37→ print(plots$river_mile_plot)\r\n 38→}\r\n 39→\r\n 40→# source table download function\r\n 41→source(\"functions/table_download.R\")\r\n 42→download_tbl(char = characteristic)\r\n 43→\r\n 44→# source threshold table function\r\n 45→source(\"functions/threshold_table.R\")\r\n 46→show_threshold_table(characteristic)\r\n 47→\r\n 48→```\r\n 49→" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } }, { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01EK555G68682oGUgF5fVwD9", "toolName": "read", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[File: 3.2KB, 53 lines, showing lines 1-53]\n 1→# Iron\r\n 2→\r\n 3→---\r\n 4→execute:\r\n 5→ echo: false\r\n 6→date: \"`r Sys.Date()`\"\r\n 7→format:\r\n 8→ html:\r\n 9→ code-fold: true\r\n 10→ code-tools: true\r\n 11→ code-summary: \"Show the code\"\r\n 12→---\r\n 13→\r\n 14→Naturally present in many rocks and soils, iron is required by plants and animals for metabolism (Glass, 2001). Sources of detrimental levels of iron are industrial waste, mining, and iron-rich groundwater, and when high concentrations of iron react with dissolved oxygen, precipitates form that can harm salmon eggs and other aquatic life (USEPA, 1976). The ADEC and the USEPA have set the iron standard for the chronic exposure of freshwater aquatic life at 1 mg/L (see Appendix X) (ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 2014).\r\n 15→\r\n 16→In the mainstem, the highest concentration of iron was 128 mg/L, which occurred at Mile 6.5 during spring 2006, and 0.03 mg/L was the lowest concentration that occurred at Mile 70 during spring 2013 (Table X). Mile 1.5 and Mile 6.5 had the highest medians during the spring and the summer (Figures X & X). Mile 1.5 and Mile 6.5 both exceeded the standard in the majority of samples (Table 13). In the spring, the median at Mile 10.1 also exceeded the standard, but the medians from all other mainstem locations were below the standard in both the spring and the summer (Figures X & X). There was a general upward trend in iron concentration from Kenai Lake to the estuary, especially in the summer.\r\n 17→\r\n 18→The concentrations in the tributaries ranged from a high of 20.5 mg/L in Beaver Creek in spring 2006 to Russian River, which had the lowest concentration at below the MDL of 0.0027 mg/L (Table X). No Name Creek, Beaver Creek, Slikok Creek, Soldotna Creek, Funny River and Moose River all had medians exceeding the standard in Spring (Figure 94). No Name Creek and Beaver Creek had medians exceeding the standards during summer (Figure X). Russian River had the lowest iron levels with most of the samples reported below the MDL or MRL. In both the tributaries and the mainstem, iron levels were higher in the spring than in the summer (Figures X-X).\r\n 19→\r\n 20→```{r echo = F, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,fig.height=8, fig.width=10}\r\n 21→\r\n 22→# set parameter\r\n 23→characteristic <- \"Iron\"\r\n 24→\r\n 25→# Define the sample fraction filter\r\n 26→sample_fraction <- c(\"Total Recoverable\", \"Unfiltered\")\r\n 27→\r\n 28→# source static regulatory criteria boxplot function\r\n 29→source(\"functions/static_boxplot_function.R\")\r\n 30→\r\n 31→# Render plots directly in chunk — htmlwidgets must be output from direct chunk code in Quarto; printing inside source() is not captured for widget output.\r\n 32→if (knitr::is_html_output()) {\r\n 33→ htmltools::tagList(\r\n 34→ plotly::ggplotly(plots$tributary_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 700) |> clean_plotly_legend(),\r\n 35→ plotly::ggplotly(plots$river_mile_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 550) |> clean_plotly_legend()\r\n 36→ )\r\n 37→} else {\r\n 38→ print(plots$tributary_plot)\r\n 39→ print(plots$river_mile_plot)\r\n 40→}\r\n 41→\r\n 42→# source table download function\r\n 43→source(\"functions/table_download.R\")\r\n 44→download_tbl(char = characteristic)\r\n 45→\r\n 46→# source threshold table function\r\n 47→source(\"functions/threshold_table.R\")\r\n 48→show_threshold_table(characteristic)\r\n 49→\r\n 50→# need to distinguish between total vs. field filtered (eg dissolved); (specified in ResultSampleFractionText). maybe a seperate plot or table to compare values. \r\n 51→\r\n 52→```\r\n 53→" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "Now 57→" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } }, { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01U9qm7dCXg8qN74qyKfQS6m", "toolName": "read", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[File: 5.2KB, 138 lines, showing lines 1-138]\n 1→# threshold_table.R\r\n 2→# Renders a summary table of regulatory threshold values for a given characteristic.\r\n 3→# Called from each parameter chapter after the CSV download link.\r\n 4→#\r\n 5→# For static thresholds, numeric values come from all_reg_vals.csv.\r\n 6→# For hardness-dependent metals (Cd, Cr, Cu, Pb, Zn), the table shows the range\r\n 7→# of calculated threshold values across all samples in the full dataset\r\n 8→# (calculated_metals_reg_vals.csv), reflecting the range of observed hardness.\r\n 9→# This gives readers actionable numerical context without requiring formula notation.\r\n 10→#\r\n 11→# Usage (in each parameter chapter, after source(\"functions/table_download.R\")):\r\n 12→# source(\"functions/threshold_table.R\")\r\n 13→# show_threshold_table(characteristic)\r\n 14→\r\n 15→library(dplyr)\r\n 16→library(knitr)\r\n 17→\r\n 18→show_threshold_table <- function(characteristic, no_threshold_note = NULL) {\r\n 19→\r\n 20→ # --- Lookup tables: Standard code -> display label and regulatory authority ---\r\n 21→ standard_labels <- c(\r\n 22→ drinking_water = \"Drinking water (MCL)\",\r\n 23→ stock_water = \"Stock water\",\r\n 24→ irrigation_water = \"Irrigation water\",\r\n 25→ aquaculture_maximum_water = \"Aquaculture (maximum pH)\",\r\n 26→ aquaculture_minimum_water = \"Aquaculture (minimum pH)\",\r\n 27→ aquaculture_water = \"Aquaculture\",\r\n 28→ wildlife = \"Wildlife\",\r\n 29→ recreation = \"Recreation\"\r\n 30→ )\r\n 31→\r\n 32→ standard_authority <- c(\r\n 33→ drinking_water = \"USEPA\",\r\n 34→ stock_water = \"ADEC\",\r\n 35→ irrigation_water = \"ADEC\",\r\n 36→ aquaculture_maximum_water = \"ADEC\",\r\n 37→ aquaculture_minimum_water = \"ADEC\",\r\n 38→ aquaculture_water = \"ADEC\",\r\n 39→ wildlife = \"ADEC\",\r\n 40→ recreation = \"ADEC\"\r\n 41→ )\r\n 42→\r\n 43→ unit_labels <- c(\r\n 44→ \"ug/l\" = \"\\u00b5g/L\",\r\n 45→ \"mg/l\" = \"mg/L\",\r\n 46→ \"none\" = \"\\u2014\" # em dash for dimensionless (e.g. pH)\r\n 47→ )\r\n 48→\r\n 49→ # --- Static thresholds ---\r\n 50→ reg_vals <- read.csv(\"other/output/regulatory_values/all_reg_vals.csv\",\r\n 51→ stringsAsFactors = FALSE)\r\n 52→\r\n 53→ static_rows <- reg_vals |>\r\n 54→ filter(characteristic_name == characteristic, !is.na(value)) |>\r\n 55→ mutate(\r\n 56→ standard_type = dplyr::coalesce(standard_labels[Standard], Standard),\r\n 57→ authority = dplyr::coalesce(standard_authority[Standard], \"\"),\r\n 58→ unit_display = dplyr::coalesce(unit_labels[tolower(reg_unit)], reg_unit),\r\n 59→ value_fmt = format(value, scientific = FALSE, drop0trailing = TRUE) |>\r\n 60→ trimws()\r\n 61→ ) |>\r\n 62→ select(\r\n 63→ `Standard Type` = standard_type,\r\n 64→ `Value` = value_fmt,\r\n 65→ `Unit` = unit_display,\r\n 66→ `Regulatory Authority` = authority\r\n 67→ )\r\n 68→\r\n 69→ # --- Hardness-dependent thresholds ---\r\n 70→ calc_path <- \"other/input/regulatory_limits/formatted_reg_vals/calculated_metals_reg_vals.csv\"\r\n 71→ hd_rows <- data.frame(\r\n 72→ `Standard Type` = character(),\r\n 73→ `Value` = character(),\r\n 74→ `Unit` = character(),\r\n 75→ `Regulatory Authority` = character(),\r\n 76→ check.names = FALSE\r\n 77→ )\r\n 78→\r\n 79→ if (file.exists(calc_path)) {\r\n 80→ calc <- read.csv(calc_path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)\r\n 81→ hd_data <- calc |>\r\n 82→ filter(characteristic_name == characteristic)\r\n 83→\r\n 84→ # Acute criterion\r\n 85→ acute_vals <- hd_data$fw_acute_std[!is.na(hd_data$fw_acute_std) &\r\n 86→ is.finite(hd_data$fw_acute_std)]\r\n 87→ if (length(acute_vals) > 0) {\r\n 88→ hd_rows <- rbind(hd_rows, data.frame(\r\n 89→ `Standard Type` = \"Aquatic life \\u2013 acute (hardness-dependent)\",\r\n 90→ `Value` = paste0(\r\n 91→ signif(min(acute_vals), 3), \" \\u2013 \", signif(max(acute_vals), 3)\r\n 92→ ),\r\n 93→ `Unit` = \"\\u00b5g/L\",\r\n 94→ `Regulatory Authority` = \"USEPA\",\r\n 95→ check.names = FALSE\r\n 96→ ))\r\n 97→ }\r\n 98→\r\n 99→ # Chronic criterion\r\n 100→ chronic_vals <- hd_data$fw_chronic_std[!is.na(hd_data$fw_chronic_std) &\r\n 101→ is.finite(hd_data$fw_chronic_std)]\r\n 102→ if (length(chronic_vals) > 0) {\r\n 103→ hd_rows <- rbind(hd_rows, data.frame(\r\n 104→ `Standard Type` = \"Aquatic life \\u2013 chronic (hardness-dependent)\",\r\n 105→ `Value` = paste0(\r\n 106→ signif(min(chronic_vals), 3), \" \\u2013 \", signif(max(chronic_vals), 3)\r\n 107→ ),\r\n 108→ `Unit` = \"\\u00b5g/L\",\r\n 109→ `Regulatory Authority` = \"USEPA\",\r\n 110→ check.names = FALSE\r\n 111→ ))\r\n 112→ }\r\n 113→ }\r\n 114→\r\n 115→ # --- Combine and render ---\r\n 116→ all_rows <- rbind(static_rows, hd_rows)\r\n 117→\r\n 118→ if (nrow(all_rows) == 0) {\r\n 119→ # Use custom note if provided, otherwise use the default message\r\n 120→ msg <- if (!is.null(no_threshold_note)) {\r\n 121→ no_threshold_note\r\n 122→ } else {\r\n 123→ paste0(\"*No regulatory threshold for \", characteristic,\r\n 124→ \" has been established for freshwater aquatic life by ADEC or USEPA.*\")\r\n 125→ }\r\n 126→ return(knitr::asis_output(msg))\r\n 127→ }\r\n 128→\r\n 129→ knitr::kable(\r\n 130→ all_rows,\r\n 131→ caption = paste(\"Regulatory thresholds for\", characteristic,\r\n 132→ \"(hardness-dependent ranges reflect observed hardness across\",\r\n 133→ \"all dataset years)\"),\r\n 134→ col.names = c(\"Standard Type\", \"Value\", \"Unit\", \"Regulatory Authority\"),\r\n 135→ align = c(\"l\", \"r\", \"l\", \"l\")\r\n 136→ )\r\n 137→}\r\n 138→" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "Now I have a clear picture. Let me plan what I need to do:\n\n1. **Water Temperature thresholds** - Add to `all_reg_vals.csv`:\n - 13°C - ADEC - egg/fry incubation and spawning areas\n - 15°C - ADEC - rearing areas and migration routes\n - 20°C - ADEC - all freshwaters\n - Units: °C, standard types need to be created\n - Also update `threshold_table.R` to handle these new standard type labels\n - Update `water_temp.qmd` narrative text to reflect the use of loggers and note these standards\n\n\n2. **Fecal Coliform** - I need to add the ADEC recreation and drinking water limits to `all_reg_vals.csv`, update the threshold table to handle CFU/100mL units, and revise the narrative to clarify that we're only sampling twice per summer and that a single exceedance could trigger additional monitoring.\n3. **Iron citations** - The current citation needs attention.\n\nUpdating the iron citation to point to the current EPA NRWQC page instead of the outdated 2008 and 2014 references.\n\n4. **How's My Waterway visibility** - I need to verify that the data pipeline will properly surface our data in the app.\n\nNow I'm working through the water temperature thresholds and the standard type codes I need to add for different freshwater conditions.\n\nFor the fecal coliform standards, I'm identifying the threshold values and their corresponding standard types for recreation and drinking water contexts.\n\nNow I'm looking at the iron citations and considering whether the EPA NRWQC reference needs updating, though I'd need to verify the current URL format if there's a references file to check. I'm realizing the citation year 2014 is still valid since it refers to when the EPA's National Recommended Water Quality Criteria table was last substantially updated, even though the URL has changed. The main improvement would be updating to a more current or specific citation format that reflects the current EPA page location. I need to track down the references file to verify the current citation format, and then consider whether to update the iron criterion year to 1976 (the original source) or to a more recent NRWQC version. For the How's My Waterway issue, the 2021 data might be delayed in propagating through the system, or there could be formatting problems preventing it from displaying properly.\n\nBut wait—the user mentioned that even historical data isn't showing up, which points to something more systemic. For How's My Waterway to display data, it needs to be properly formatted in WQX with correct organization and monitoring location information that aligns with the National Hydrography Dataset. If the data exists on the Water Quality Portal but isn't appearing in HMW, the issue likely stems from how the monitoring locations are mapped to NHD waterbodies or from missing data completeness requirements.\n\nLet me start by examining the upload documentation and the CSV files to understand the current pipeline, then cross-reference that against HMW's visibility requirements.", } } }, { "type": "text", "text": "Let me read a few more files before proposing changes." }, { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_018grindRDgWUyjzT6YT7Wmo", "toolName": "read", "input": { "file_path": "D:\\GitHub\\kenai-river-wqx\\cdx_upload.qmd" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } }, { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_01NhJhtj2VhYSaFMxbSxAgXo", "toolName": "bash", "input": { "command": "head -5 \"D:\\GitHub\\kenai-river-wqx\\other\\output\\wqx_formatted\\results_activities.csv\" 2>/dev/null || head -5 \"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx/other/output/wqx_formatted/results_activities.csv\"", "intent": "Check the structure of the WQX results_activities.csv export" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } }, { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_01PXZ1FmEFchrQbGDj6WNcwM", "toolName": "bash", "input": { "command": "head -5 \"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx/other/output/wqx_formatted/station.csv\"", "intent": "Check station.csv structure" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ], "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 3621, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 2398, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 3621, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null }, "positai": { "timestamp": 1774995620115, "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6", "modelName": "Claude Sonnet 4.6", "providerId": "positai", "usage": { "inputTokens": 1, "outputTokens": 2398, "totalTokens": 40761, "cacheReadTokens": 34741, "cacheWriteTokens": 3621, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 3621, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_018grindRDgWUyjzT6YT7Wmo", "toolName": "read", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[File: 10.3KB, 192 lines, showing lines 1-192]\n 1→---\n 2→title: \"EPA CDX Upload and Data Management Procedures\"\n 3→---\n 4→\n 5→```{r setup, include=FALSE}\n 6→knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)\n 7→```\n 8→\n 9→# EPA CDX Upload and Data Management Procedures {#cdx-upload}\n 10→\n 11→This chapter documents the step-by-step procedures for submitting Kenai River baseline water quality data to the EPA Central Data Exchange (CDX) / Water Quality Exchange (WQX), and for removing or correcting previously submitted data.\n 12→\n 13→> **Note:** This chapter was developed and tested during the 2021 data submission (completed March 31, 2026). Steps reflect what was actually confirmed to work — not inferred or assumed. Where a step has not been personally verified, it is noted.\n 14→\n 15→**CDX login URL:** \n 16→\n 17→**KWF Organization ID in EPA WQX:** `KENAI_WQX`\n 18→\n 19→**EPA WQX support contact:** wqx\\@epa.gov / 800-424-9067\n 20→\n 21→The three output files ready for upload are generated by `appendix_a.qmd` and written to `other/output/wqx_formatted/`:\n 22→\n 23→| File | Contents |\n 24→|----|----|\n 25→| `results_activities.csv` | All sample results and associated activity metadata |\n 26→| `project.csv` | Project description and QAPP approval status |\n 27→| `station.csv` | Monitoring location metadata |\n 28→\n 29→Upload these files together in the same CDX session. Also have the QAPP PDF on hand for the project upload:\n 30→\n 31→`KenaiWatershedForum_QAPP_v3_2023_with_Addendum_April_2024.pdf`\n 32→\n 33→------------------------------------------------------------------------\n 34→\n 35→## Uploading Data to EPA CDX {#sec-upload}\n 36→\n 37→*The general sequence below is confirmed correct. Exact button labels should be verified against the WQX Web interface at time of upload — the UI may change between annual submissions.*\n 38→\n 39→### Step 1: Log in to CDX\n 40→\n 41→1. Navigate to .\n 42→2. Log in with your CDX username and password.\n 43→3. From the **My CDX** dashboard, locate **WQX Web** under \"My Programs\" and click **Go to WQX Web**.\n 44→\n 45→### Step 2: Upload the Station file\n 46→\n 47→The station file registers monitoring location metadata (coordinates, location descriptions, etc.). Upload this before results, as results reference station IDs.\n 48→\n 49→1. In WQX Web, navigate to **Import & Submit**.\n 50→2. Select import type: **Monitoring Location**.\n 51→3. Select the KWF station import configuration (or create one — see the [WQX Web User Guide](https://www.epa.gov/waterdata/water-quality-exchange-web-wqx-web-user-guide)).\n 52→4. Browse to and select `other/output/wqx_formatted/station.csv`.\n 53→5. Submit and wait for **Completed** status.\n 54→\n 55→### Step 3: Upload the Project file {#sec-upload-project}\n 56→\n 57→1. In WQX Web, navigate to **Import & Submit**.\n 58→2. Select import type: **Project**.\n 59→3. Select the KWF project import configuration.\n 60→4. Browse to and select `other/output/wqx_formatted/project.csv`.\n 61→5. Attach the QAPP PDF (`KenaiWatershedForum_QAPP_v3_2023_with_Addendum_April_2024.pdf`) and set attachment type to **QAPP**.\n 62→6. Submit and wait for **Completed** status.\n 63→\n 64→### Step 4: Upload the Results and Activities file\n 65→\n 66→1. In WQX Web, navigate to **Import & Submit**.\n 67→2. Select import type: **Activity** (results are nested under activities in WQX).\n 68→3. Select the KWF results/activities import configuration.\n 69→4. Browse to and select `other/output/wqx_formatted/results_activities.csv`.\n 70→5. Submit and wait for **Completed** status. This is the largest file and may take several minutes.\n 71→\n 72→### Step 5: Verify the upload\n 73→\n 74→1. Allow 24–48 hours for data to propagate, then search for KWF data in [How's My Waterway](https://mywaterway.epa.gov/) or the [Water Quality Portal](https://www.waterqualitydata.us/).\n 75→2. Confirm that results are visible and that values, units, and site names look correct.\n 76→\n 77→------------------------------------------------------------------------\n 78→\n 79→## Deleting Previously Submitted Data {#sec-delete}\n 80→\n 81→Deletion is necessary when Activity IDs change between submissions (e.g., due to corrected analyte naming conventions). Re-uploading alone only overwrites records with exactly matching Activity IDs — records with changed IDs remain as orphans in WQX. See @sec-overwrite for when re-upload without deletion is sufficient.\n 82→\n 83→> **Important:** Deletion in WQX is permanent. Always confirm the scope before proceeding and keep local copies of all submission files.\n 84→\n 85→### Generating a delete file\n 86→\n 87→The delete file is a single-column CSV listing the Activity IDs to remove. Activity IDs must be provided **without** the organization prefix — WQX resolves them against the organization associated with the logged-in account.\n 88→\n 89→Activity IDs as downloaded from the Water Quality Portal include the org prefix (e.g., `KENAI_WQX-KBL_t_30.0-2021-07-27-P`). Strip the prefix before using them in a delete file:\n 90→\n 91→``` r\n 92→library(tidyverse)\n 93→\n 94→# Load WQP download and extract 2021 Activity IDs\n 95→wqp <- read_csv(\"other/input/WQX_downloads/wqp_download_YYYYMMDD/narrowresult.csv\")\n 96→\n 97→delete_file <- wqp |>\n 98→ filter(year(ActivityStartDate) == 2021) |>\n 99→ select(ActivityIdentifier) |>\n 100→ distinct() |>\n 101→ mutate(ActivityIdentifier = sub(\"^KENAI_WQX-\", \"\", ActivityIdentifier))\n 102→\n 103→write_csv(delete_file, \"other/output/epa_wqp_uploads/corrected_epa_wqp_uploads/resultphyschem_DELETE.csv\")\n 104→```\n 105→\n 106→The resulting file should have a single column named `ActivityIdentifier` with one ID per row (no org prefix).\n 107→\n 108→### Delete UI path in WQX Web (confirmed working 3/31/2026)\n 109→\n 110→1. Log in to CDX and navigate to WQX Web.\n 111→2. Go to **Import & Submit** → **Import a batch of IDs for records to delete from WQX** → **Import a file of Activity IDs to be deleted**.\n 112→3. Set **Type of File** to `CSV (Comma delimited)`.\n 113→4. Leave **\"Ignore First Row of Import File?\"** checked — the file has a header row that should be skipped.\n 114→5. Confirm that **Organization ID** is auto-populated as `KENAI_WQX` from your login.\n 115→6. Browse to and select the delete CSV file.\n 116→7. Submit and monitor until completed.\n 117→\n 118→> **UI note:** The WQX Web interface labels this import type as **\"Activity Group IDs to Delete\"**, which is potentially confusing — it correctly deletes individual Activity records (not Activity Groups, which are a separate WQX concept). This label is accurate as of March 2026.\n 119→\n 120→### Verifying the delete\n 121→\n 122→After the import completes, download a fresh results file from the Water Quality Portal for organization `KENAI_WQX` and the relevant date range. Confirm zero records are returned before proceeding with the re-upload.\n 123→\n 124→------------------------------------------------------------------------\n 125→\n 126→## Updating Data Without Full Deletion {#sec-overwrite}\n 127→\n 128→If the Activity IDs in the corrected file exactly match what is already in WQX (i.e., only *values* are changing, not the IDs themselves), a full delete is not necessary. Re-uploading a file with matching Activity IDs will overwrite the existing records for those IDs.\n 129→\n 130→Use this approach only when you are confident no Activity IDs have changed. If analyte naming conventions, site codes, or sample type suffixes have been revised, the ID structure may have changed — use the full delete approach instead.\n 131→\n 132→To check for ID mismatches between your corrected file and WQX before deciding:\n 133→\n 134→``` r\n 135→# Load WQP download and strip prefix\n 136→wqx_ids <- read_csv(\"other/input/WQX_downloads/wqp_download_YYYYMMDD/narrowresult.csv\") |>\n 137→ filter(year(ActivityStartDate) == 2021) |>\n 138→ distinct(ActivityIdentifier) |>\n 139→ mutate(ID_stripped = sub(\"^KENAI_WQX-\", \"\", ActivityIdentifier))\n 140→\n 141→# Load corrected upload file\n 142→new_ids <- read_csv(\"other/output/wqx_formatted/results_activities.csv\") |>\n 143→ distinct(`Activity ID`)\n 144→\n 145→cat(\"WQX records not matched by new file (would become orphans):\",\n 146→ sum(!wqx_ids$ID_stripped %in% new_ids$`Activity ID`), \"\\n\")\n 147→```\n 148→\n 149→If this count is zero, re-upload without deletion. If nonzero, use the full delete approach.\n 150→\n 151→------------------------------------------------------------------------\n 152→\n 153→## WQX Data Structure Note {#sec-structure}\n 154→\n 155→As of March 2026, KWF's WQX data is organized at the **Activity level**: each Activity record contains one result. EPA WQX support (Kevin Christian, wqx\\@epa.gov) recommends organizing data at the **Results level** instead, where each Activity represents one sampling event (unique location + date) and multiple Results are nested under it.\n 156→\n 157→The current structure is not invalid, but performance may degrade as the total record count grows. Restructuring involves using the **Review** function in WQX Web to re-define existing records. This is a lower-priority task best addressed in the `kenai-river-wqx-qaqc` repo and incorporated into future annual submission pipelines.\n 158→\n 159→------------------------------------------------------------------------\n 160→\n 161→## Removing the Existing 2021 KWF Submission (Reference — Completed 3/31/2026)\n 162→\n 163→The 2021 KWF dataset was fully deleted on 3/31/2026 and replaced with a corrected version. The corrected files are:\n 164→\n 165→- `other/output/wqx_formatted/results_activities.csv` (843 records)\n 166→- `other/output/wqx_formatted/project.csv`\n 167→- `other/output/wqx_formatted/station.csv`\n 168→\n 169→Corrections addressed: Ca/Mg/Fe unit errors (summer 2021 SGS EDD reported dissolved metals as µg/L when values were on mg/L scale); RM 1.5 site ID mismatch; renamed Activity IDs (`Nitrate_Nitrite-N` → `Total Nitrate/Nitrite-N`, `Total xylenes` → individual xylene names). See @appendix_a for full QA/QC details.\n 170→\n 171→------------------------------------------------------------------------\n 172→\n 173→## Troubleshooting Common Issues\n 174→\n 175→| Issue | Likely Cause | Resolution |\n 176→|----|----|----|\n 177→| Delete import fails: \"Domain Value Invalid\" for all rows | Activity IDs include the `KENAI_WQX-` org prefix | Strip the prefix; WQX resolves IDs against the logged-in org |\n 178→| Import fails: \"invalid monitoring location\" | Station not yet registered | Upload `station.csv` before `results_activities.csv` |\n 179→| Records submitted but not visible in WQP | Propagation delay, or status is \"Accepted\" not \"Completed\" | Wait 24–48 hours; recheck submission status |\n 180→| Re-upload leaves stale records | Activity IDs changed between versions | Use full delete approach; do not rely on overwrite alone |\n 181→\n 182→------------------------------------------------------------------------\n 183→\n 184→## EPA and WQX Resources\n 185→\n 186→- **CDX login:** \n 187→- **WQX Web user guide:** \n 188→- **WQX domain values:** \n 189→- **Water Quality Portal:** \n 190→- **How's My Waterway:** \n 191→- **EPA WQX support:** Kevin Christian — wqx\\@epa.gov / storet\\@epa.gov / 800-424-9067\n 192→" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } }, { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": The station.csv has `MonitoringLocationIdentifier` like \"KENAI_WQX-10000002\"\n2. The results reference these same IDs\n\n\n3. The HUC8 is 19020302, which is correct for the Kenai watershed\n\nThe WQP query should be pulling data from this org. Since the user mentioned the 2021 data just uploaded isn't showing up, along with historical data, this could be a propagation delay - WQP data can take days or weeks to appear in HMW - or there might be something structural preventing visibility.\n\nLooking at the monitoring location identifiers, they appear consistent between the CSV files (KENAI_WQX format), but the real issue might be that the locations need NHD reach codes to be discoverable in HMW's waterbody-organized system. The older historical data might not have been processed through the current pipeline at all.\n\nI should recommend waiting 24-72 hours for HMW propagation, verifying the data shows up in WQP first, confirming the monitoring locations have proper NHD reach associations, and checking whether the historical data even appears in WQP to begin with.\n\nThe real issue is determining whether the historical data is missing from WQP entirely or just from HMW—that distinction tells me whether this is a propagation delay or a data formatting problem. I also need to look at what format the historical data was originally uploaded in and check any reference files for citation conventions.\n\nNow I'm planning the specific changes needed...\n\nAdding five new rows to the registry with temperature thresholds for different freshwater conditions and fecal coliform standards for recreation and drinking water, using the appropriate units. Then updating the threshold table to include these new temperature categories and the aquatic life chronic standard in the labels.\n\nI'm also realizing I need to add `aquatic_life_chronic` to the standard labels since the iron standard from last session references it but it's not in the lookup table yet. Then I'll move on to updating the narrative in the water temperature document.\n\nFor the fecal coliform section, I need to clarify that we're only collecting two events and note that a single high reading could trigger additional monitoring. I should also make the criterion description more precise about what was and wasn't evaluated.\n\nFor the iron citations, I need to replace the 2014 EPA reference with something more current and accurate—likely pointing directly to the NRWQC page rather than an older summary.\n\nNow I'm tracking down the proper citation for the iron criterion. The 1 mg/L value comes from the 1976 Red Book, but I should verify whether to keep the existing ADEC/USEPA citations or update them to point to the current EPA NRWQC page. "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "So the `references.qmd` is just a placeholder for auto-generated Quarto bibliography references. The parameter chapters use inline text citations that don't link to the .bib file. So updating citations in those chapters means updating the text directly.\n\nNow let me formulate my specific proposals for each task before implementing:\n\n### Task 1: Water Temperature thresholds\n\n**`all_reg_vals.csv` additions:**\n- `\"Temperature, water\"`, `deg_c`, `temp_all_freshwaters`, 20, NA\n- `\"Temperature, water\"`, `deg_c`, `temp_rearing_migration`, 15, NA \n\n\n- `\"Temperature, water\"`, `deg_c`, `temp_egg_fry_spawning`, 13, NA\n\n**`threshold_table.R` updates:**\nAdding the new temperature thresholds to the standard labels mapping, plus fixing the iron label for aquatic life chronic standards...\n\n- Add to `unit_labels`:\n - `\"deg_c\" = \"°C\"`\n\n**`water_temp.qmd` narrative:**\nThe document has a clear structure with newer logger content at the top and older 2016 material below marked as in-progress. I need to refresh the narrative to weave in the regulatory standards context while preserving the AKTEMP and logger information, then modernize the regulatory language without losing the substantive data points from the original text.\n\nFor the water temperature section, I'll revise the opening to address temperature regime complexity, maintain the existing logger and AKTEMP context, clarify that point observations need contextual evaluation, integrate the ADEC standards appropriately, and acknowledge that while continuous logger data is valuable, ADEC standards remain the regulatory baseline.\n\n### Task 2: Fecal Coliform thresholds\n\nNow I'm adding the fecal coliform entries to the regulatory values table with the recreation and drinking water single-sample thresholds...\n\nUpdating the threshold table with the new standard labels for single-sample limits, assigning both to ADEC authority, and adding the CFU/100 mL unit label...\n\nFor the fecal coliform narrative, I need to clarify that monitoring happens at two discrete events annually rather than continuous sampling, establish that single-sample limits are what applies to this dataset, and note that exceeding the single-sample threshold is the relevant finding.\n\nOn the iron citation, I'm weighing whether to keep the 2014 EPA reference since that's when the NRWQC table was last checked, cite the original 1976 source where the criterion actually comes from, or update to a more recent version if available. Looking at the NRWQC table, I see that iron was removed from EPA's standard criteria after a review, so the 1 mg/L criterion actually originates from EPA's 1976 \"Quality Criteria for Water\" document, which Alaska later adopted. The user wants me to refresh the citations in the iron chapter, so I should update the \"(USEPA, 2014)\" reference to point to the correct 1976 source or note that it's from the current EPA page with proper attribution to the original standard. I'm going in circles here—the core issue is just updating the citation to reflect the current EPA source accurately. I'll simplify this: keep the ADEC reference as-is and update the EPA citation to \"(USEPA, 1976)\" since that's the original source document for the iron criterion, which is cleaner than trying to cite multiple years. I'm realizing the iron criterion of 1 mg/L actually isn't in EPA's current recommended criteria table—EPA removed iron from their list in recent updates—so the 1976 USEPA guidance and Alaska's 18 AAC 70 adoption would be more accurate citations than the 2014 reference. Let me check what the document currently cites to make the most defensible update. I'm realizing the user wants me to update the citation to reflect the current EPA reference rather than just fix the year. Since the iron criterion comes from EPA's National Recommended Water Quality Criteria, I should cite the most current version available, which would be USEPA (2024) pointing to their current criteria table.\n\nNow I have a clear implementation plan: I need to add five new rows to the regulatory values file for temperature and fecal coliform standards, update the threshold table script to include the missing aquatic life chronic label and add unit conversions, modernize the water temperature narrative to reflect current logger practices, and clarify the fecal coliform sampling frequency language.\n\nFor the How's My Waterway issue, the 2021 data upload is likely just experiencing normal propagation delays—data typically takes a day or two to move through WQX to WQP before HMW picks it up on its own refresh cycle. The historical data not appearing is more puzzling and worth investigating further to understand what the user means by \"historical.\" The most likely culprits are propagation delay from today's upload, a geographic mismatch where HMW's NHD waterbody filter doesn't align with the monitoring locations, or the Kenai watershed's HUC (19020302) not being indexed in HMW's system. I should verify the data directly in WQP to rule out organization ID mismatches.\n\nGiven what the user has already confirmed about the three parameter updates, I'll move forward with implementing those changes and then investigate the HMW data availability issue—they've already approved the direction, so I don't need to re-confirm before proceeding. I'm adding the new standard labels for temperature thresholds and fecal coliform limits, then updating the authority mappings to reflect ADEC and USEPA sources for these standards.\n\nI'll also add the unit label conversions for degrees Celsius and CFU measurements.\n\nFor the narrative updates, I'm modernizing the water temperature introduction to include ADEC standards and logger context, then revising the fecal coliform section to clarify that monitoring happens twice yearly rather than continuously, explain why the geometric mean can't be assessed with this design, and note that single-sample exceedances warrant further investigation while keeping the existing data discussion intact.\n\nNow I need to verify the EPA iron criterion citation—the 1976 Red Book is the original source for the 1 mg/L standard, though ADEC 2008 also references it. "type": "web_search_result" } ] } }, { "type": "reasoning", "text": "Good. From the search results, I can confirm:\n1. EPA's 1976 \"Red Book\" established the chronic aquatic life criterion for iron, and the freshwater aquatic life criterion for iron is a chronic criterion of 1000 µg/L (1.0 mg/L).\n\n2. The iron criterion is not currently in EPA's main NRWQC table (it wasn't revised and isn't in the 2002+ compilations). Water quality criteria that predate EPA's 1980 human health methodology were not revised for this effort, and EPA believes revisions for these criteria, which include iron, may warrant major reassessments.\n \nNow I see the 2014 citation was just pointing to a summary page that referenced these older criteria. The actual source I need is the 1976 Red Book for the iron aquatic life criterion. So I should update the citation in the iron chapter from the 2014 EPA reference to USEPA (1976), while keeping the Alaska standard citation as is.\n\nI have what I need to make these updates. I'm going to add the five new rows for temperature and fecal coliform standards to the regulatory values file, then update the threshold table accordingly.\n\nFor the threshold table, I'm adding the lookup entries that map these new standard codes to their human-readable labels and authorities. The temperature standards are all from ADEC, while the fecal coliform standards come from ADEC and USEPA.\n\nNow I need to update the narrative in the water temperature chapter to introduce these regulatory standards properly, keeping the existing context about temperature complexity and the logger data while replacing the placeholder text with actual standard definitions.\n\nFor the fecal coliform section, I'm clarifying that the monitoring program's twice-yearly sampling schedule doesn't align with the 30-day geometric mean standard, so I need to focus on the single-sample limits that actually apply to this dataset instead.\n\nFor iron, I'm correcting the EPA citation year from 2014 back to 1976 to reflect when the criterion was originally established in the Red Book.", The 2014 date in the current citation was pointing to the NRWQC summary page, but that page no longer includes iron (iron predates EPA's 1980 methodology and has not been revised). \nInteresting - the file already says \"(USEPA, 1976)\" in the middle of the paragraph for one citation, and \"(ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 2014)\" at the end. I need to update the latter citation to use 1976. But wait - these are two different citations in the same sentence:\n1. \"...precipitates form that can harm salmon eggs and other aquatic life (USEPA, 1976)\" - this is already correct\n2. \"...at 1 mg/L (see Appendix X) (ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 2014)\" - this needs to be updated\n\n\nSince the 1 mg/L criterion actually comes from the 1976 Red Book and isn't in the current NRWQC table, I should change the second citation to \"(ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 1976)\" - the ADEC manual cites this criterion too, so both sources are accurate.\n
\n\n\n\n

\n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\nAll five changes are done. Here's a brief summary:\n\n**Files changed:**\n\n- **`all_reg_vals.csv`** — 5 new rows added (rows 56–60).\n- **`threshold_table.R`** — added `standard_labels`, `standard_authority`, and `unit_labels` entries for all new standard types, plus `aquatic_life_chronic` which was missing and would have displayed the raw code string in the iron table.\n- **`water_temp.qmd`** — removed the \"Writing in progress\" placeholder; combined the logger/AKTEMP context with the ADEC standards (20/15/13°C) and a note that grab samples should be read alongside continuous records.\n- **`fecal_coliform.qmd`** — rewrote the opening paragraph. The old text explained the 30-day geometric mean criteria at length and then noted they weren't evaluated. The new version leads with the reason (two events per year, not a 30-day sample series), names the single-sample limits as the applicable comparison, and explicitly says an exceedance should prompt additional monitoring rather than be treated as a standard violation.\n- **`iron.qmd`** — `(USEPA, 2014)` → `(USEPA, 1976)`. The 1 mg/L criterion was established in EPA's 1976 \"Quality Criteria for Water\" (Red Book) and has not been revised in EPA's current NRWQC table, so the 1976 date is more accurate.\n\n---\n\n**On the How's My Waterway question (#4):** The 2021 data was uploaded only today, and WQP/HMW propagation typically takes 24–72 hours or longer. That delay is almost certainly the reason the 2021 data isn't visible yet.\n\nFor the historical data not appearing, there are two things worth checking before drawing conclusions:\n\n1. **Does it appear in WQP first?** Go to [waterqualitydata.us](https://www.waterqualitydata.us/), search by Organization ID `KENAI_WQX`, and see if pre-2021 records are returned. If they are, the data exists in WQX but HMW may not have indexed the Kenai watershed. If they aren't, there's a deeper upload gap.\n\n2. **NHD reach mapping.** How's My Waterway displays results organized by NHD waterbody. If the KWF monitoring locations in WQX don't have NHD Reach codes attached, they may not surface in HMW even when the underlying data is in WQP. The `station.csv` as currently structured doesn't include an NHD Reach ID field. Kevin Christian at EPA WQX support would be the right person to ask whether adding NHD codes to the station file would fix HMW visibility.\n\nI'd suggest checking WQP first before assuming there's a pipeline problem — that's the more actionable starting point.\n\n\nCheck waterqualitydata.us for organization KENAI_WQX to confirm whether historical data is queryable there before investigating HMW-specific issues.\n\n\n\nVerify the threshold tables render correctly in the three updated chapters by running a quick test render of those chapters.\n\n\n\n\n" ] }, "lmMessage": [ { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "The user confirmed. \"\\u00b5g/L\",\n \"mg/l\" = \"mg/L\",\n \"none\" = \"\\u2014\" # em dash for dimensionless (e.g. pH)\n )", "new_string": " standard_labels <- c(\n drinking_water = \"Drinking water (MCL)\",\n (pending)" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } }, "databot": { "microCompactKeep": true } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_013k3Gdo5d1CrNKiEaqMWAFK", "toolName": "edit", "input": { "file_path": "D:\\GitHub\\kenai-river-wqx\\parameters\\water_temp.qmd", "old_string": "The Kenai River is a complex mosaic of water temperature regimes, from glacially-influenced streams like Ptarmigan Creek (not monitored in this study) to mountainous but mostly glacier-free watersheds like the Russian River to lowland tributaries like Beaver Creek.\n\nThe technology used to monitor environmental water temperatures has progressed remarkably since this project initiated in the year 2000. Single-point instantaneous water temperature measurements, as described in the current QAPP, will continue to be an important aspect of this work. However, stream and lake temperatures overall are better understood as continuous time series. To this end, AKTEMP, an online repository specifically designed for water temperature data, is home to Kenai Watershed Forum's water temperature monitoring efforts.\n\nSingle-point observations described here should be evaluated in context of these larger datasets where possible\n\n*(Writing in progress here 1/15/2025; old text below)*\n\nLow water temperatures between 0°C and 4°C can result in low growth rates for fish, but much higher water temperatures can encourage disease, competitors, predators, mortality, and an oxygen-deprived habitat (Kyle and Brabets, 2001). The ADEC has five temperature standards for the growth and propagation of fish, shellfish, other aquatic life, and wildlife in freshwater; the water temperature must remain below 20°C in all areas, below 15°C in rearing areas and migration routes, and below 13°C in egg and fry incubation and spawning areas (see Appendix X) (ADEC, 2012).", "new_string": "The Kenai River is a complex mosaic of water temperature regimes, from glacially-influenced streams like Ptarmigan Creek (not monitored in this study) to mountainous but mostly glacier-free watersheds like the Russian River to lowland tributaries like Beaver Creek.\n\nThe technology used to monitor environmental water temperatures has progressed remarkably since this project initiated in the year 2000. Single-point instantaneous water temperature measurements, as described in the current QAPP, will continue to be an important aspect of this work. However, stream and lake temperatures overall are better understood as continuous time series. To this end, AKTEMP, an online repository specifically designed for water temperature data, is home to Kenai Watershed Forum's water temperature monitoring efforts. Single-point observations described here should be evaluated in the context of those larger datasets where possible.\n\nLow water temperatures between 0°C and 4°C can result in low growth rates for fish, while higher temperatures can encourage disease, predators, mortality, and oxygen-depleted habitat (Kyle and Brabets, 2001). Under Alaska water quality standards (18 AAC 70), water temperature must remain below 20°C in all freshwaters, below 15°C in rearing areas and migration routes, and below 13°C in egg and fry incubation and spawning areas (ADEC, 2012). Because sampling occurs at two discrete events per year rather than continuously, exceedances of these standards in the grab sample record indicate temperature conditions worth tracking, but the fuller picture requires continuous logger data from AKTEMP." }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 291, "cache_read_input_tokens": 73086, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 291, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 803, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 291, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null
