74181, "cacheReadTokens": 73086, "cacheWriteTokens": 291, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 291, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01P967EjxtFAf7W4ZeKgJeAp", "toolName": "todoWrite", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "Todo List Updated (5 items):\n✓ Add 5 rows to all_reg_vals.csv (3 temp + 2 FC) (completed)\n✓ Update threshold_table.R with new labels, authority, and unit entries (completed)\n✓ Update water_temp.qmd narrative (completed)\n→ Update fecal_coliform.qmd narrative (in progress)\n○ Update iron.qmd citation (pending)" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } }, "databot": { "microCompactKeep": true } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_019NUekRsbuqDq2wYun5mFSk", "toolName": "edit", "input": { "file_path": "D:\\GitHub\\kenai-river-wqx\\parameters\\fecal_coliform.qmd", "old_string": "The presence of fecal coliform bacteria in surface water indicates fecal contamination from warm-blooded animals, which is linked to disease-causing viruses and bacteria (Glass, 1999). Sources of fecal coliform bacteria include waste from septic systems, domestic animals, waterfowl, and other wildlife (Glass, 1999). The ADEC and USEPA standards for fecal coliform bacteria have two types of critera, a 30-day geometric mean and a no more than 10% of the samples can exceed a specified value criteira. The geometric mean criterian was not evaluated in this study because not enough samples were collected during any 30-day period. For reference, the ADEC fecal coliform drinking water standard states that in a 30-day period, the geometric mean of samples may not exceed 20 CFU/100ml and not more than 10% of the total samples may exceed 40 CFU/100ml (ADEC's single sample limit). The ADEC fecal coliform secondary recreation standard states that in a 30-day period, the geometric mean of samples may not exceed 200 CFU/100ml and not more than 10% of the total samples may exceed 400 CFU/100ml (see Appendix X) (ADEC, 2002).", "new_string": "The presence of fecal coliform bacteria in surface water indicates fecal contamination from warm-blooded animals, which is linked to disease-causing viruses and bacteria (Glass, 1999). Sources include waste from septic systems, domestic animals, waterfowl, and other wildlife (Glass, 1999).\n\nADEC fecal coliform standards have two components: a 30-day geometric mean limit and a single-sample limit (the threshold that no more than 10% of samples in a 30-day period may exceed). This monitoring program collects two discrete samples per year — one in spring and one in summer — not a series of samples over a 30-day window. As a result, the geometric mean criterion cannot be evaluated and is not reported here. The applicable comparison for this dataset is the single-sample limit: 400 CFU/100 mL for secondary recreation use and 40 CFU/100 mL for drinking water use (ADEC, 2002). A single observation above these limits should not be interpreted as a definitive standard violation, but does warrant consideration of additional, more frequent monitoring to evaluate whether the 30-day geometric mean criterion would be exceeded under closer scrutiny." }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 292, "cache_read_input_tokens": 74214, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 292, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 672, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 292, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": "toolu_01BcDetKMQnDw5pQzCQVDLqp", "toolName": "runCode", "output": { "type": "content", "value": [ { "type": "text", "text": "Output:\n1: # Fecal Coliform Bacteria\n2: \n3: ---\n4: execute:\n5: echo: false\n6: date: \"`r Sys.Date()`\"\n7: format:\n8: html:\n9: code-fold: true\n10: code-tools: true\n11: code-summary: \"Show the code\"\n12: ---\n13: \n14: The presence of fecal coliform bacteria in surface water indicates fecal contamination from warm-blooded animals, which is linked to disease-causing viruses and bacteria (Glass, 1999). Sources of fecal coliform bacteria include waste from septic systems, domestic animals, waterfowl, and other wildlife (Glass, 1999). The ADEC and USEPA standards for fecal coliform bacteria have two types of critera, a 30-day geometric mean and a no more than 10% of the samples can exceed a specified value criteira. The geometric mean criterian was not evaluated in this study because not enough samples were collected during any 30-day period. For reference, the ADEC fecal coliform drinking water standard states that in a 30-day period, the geometric mean of samples may not exceed 20 CFU/100ml and not more than 10% of the total samples may exceed 40 CFU/100ml (ADEC’s single sample limit). The ADEC fecal coliform secondary recreation standard states that in a 30-day period, the geometric mean of samples may not exceed 200 CFU/100ml and not more than 10% of the total samples may exceed 400 CFU/100ml (see Appendix X) (ADEC, 2002).\n15: \n16: The highest level of fecal coliform recorded was 2980 CFU/100ml at Mile 6.5 during the summer 2002, however this sample may be unreliable because the duplicate sample was below the MDL of 1 CFU/100ml. The next highest recorded concentration in the mainstem was 580 CFU/100ml at Mile 6.5 during the summer of 2003, and the lowest levels were 0 CFU/100ml at several locations (Table 26). The highest median in the mainstem occurred at Mile 6.5 in the spring and at Mile 1.5 in the summer. The other medians along the mainstem were all below 10 CFU/100ml for the summer and the spring. The concentration of fecal coliform was generally higher in the summer than in the spring in the mainstem and the tributaries (Figures X - X).\n17: \n18: In the tributaries, the concentration of fecal coliform ranged from a high of 520 CFU/100ml in Soldotna Creek during spring 2001 to 0 CFU/100ml at multiple sites (Table 52). During the spring, Beaver Creek had the highest median, and during the summer, No Name Creek had the highest median. In the summer, the median concentrations were relatively high in Slikok Creek and Beaver Creek, in addition to No Name Creek. Juneau Creek, Russian River, Killey River, and Moose River all had relatively low medians during the spring and the summer. In the summer, the tributaries had higher median concentrations of fecal coliform than the mainstem, but in the spring, the levels in the tributaries and the mainstem were more similar. (Figures X - X)\n19: \n20: ```{r echo = F, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE,fig.height=8, fig.width=10}\n21: \n22: # set parameter\n23: characteristic <- \"Fecal Coliform\"\n24: \n25: # source static regulatory criteria boxplot function\n26: source(\"functions/static_boxplot_function.R\")\n27: \n28: # Render plots directly in chunk — htmlwidgets must be output from direct chunk\n29: # code in Quarto; printing inside source() is not captured for widget output.\n30: if (knitr::is_html_output()) {\n31: htmltools::tagList(\n32: plotly::ggplotly(plots$tributary_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 700) |> clean_plotly_legend(),\n33: plotly::ggplotly(plots$river_mile_plot, tooltip = \"text\", height = 550) |> clean_plotly_legend()\n34: )\n35: } else {\n36: print(plots$tributary_plot)\n37: print(plots$river_mile_plot)\n38: }\n39: \n40: # source table download function\n41: source(\"functions/table_download.R\")\n42: download_tbl(char = characteristic)\n43: \n44: # source threshold table function\n45: source(\"functions/threshold_table.R\")\n46: show_threshold_table(characteristic)\n47: \n48: ```\n\n\nExecution time: 15ms" } ] }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } }, "databot": { "microCompactKeep": true } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "The file uses different quote characters or encoding. Let me write the file directly in R to replace the first paragraph.", "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "signature": "ErcCClsIDBgCKkDVNEbmwhS1SxM0jEqUHpK3QsyYZwkBSVc18RDgMu5KzgymvwNBQgDhmN7nxBqK76mTyNWhhS23talfJNBhMVU1MhFjbGF1ZGUtc29ubmV0LTQtNjgAEgwQNsqbvHSsjrxV0VUaDD/EdRBLX/IFmxJB6yIwDgADexSExduvpO4bqFU7KlrX1uVed+zWCZYlNhpYB6KakVeo+iD2oe1BqtdPZsG7KokBd9r1IK9Ra0pKAqneo/w2tjEpCnUes4jyTkE9Ww+4iFvnVbIBkbCDN8DZVnQmTQz8y0ysVCXBFJx89qPxK4dtC6pQUvZVqxj/R+iJwLz6ekGBSi57kdTnv3a0WS1VHG983OamIOPTvWEgMG+FFTJLX7W5LCaRCe5Rx+nk2l0LLMKRUPGg2eSboQAYAQ==" } } }, { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_01PFszGSeiyzoEG86s3cFMkZ", "toolName": "runCode", "input": { "code": "lines <- readLines(\"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx/parameters/fecal_coliform.qmd\")\n\n# Replace line 14 (the old paragraph) with the new text\nnew_para <- \"The presence of fecal coliform bacteria in surface water indicates fecal contamination from warm-blooded animals, which is linked to disease-causing viruses and bacteria (Glass, 1999). Sources include waste from septic systems, domestic animals, waterfowl, and other wildlife (Glass, 1999).\n\nADEC fecal coliform standards have two components: a 30-day geometric mean limit and a single-sample limit (the threshold that no more than 10% of samples in a 30-day period may exceed). This monitoring program collects two discrete samples per year — one in spring and one in summer — not a series of samples over a 30-day window. As a result, the geometric mean criterion cannot be evaluated and is not reported here. The applicable comparison for this dataset is the single-sample limit: 400 CFU/100 mL for secondary recreation use and 40 CFU/100 mL for drinking water use (ADEC, 2002). A single observation above these limits should not be interpreted as a definitive standard violation, but does warrant consideration of additional, more frequent monitoring to evaluate whether the 30-day geometric mean criterion would be exceeded under closer scrutiny.\"\n\nlines[14] <- new_para\n\nwriteLines(lines, \"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx/parameters/fecal_coliform.qmd\")\ncat(\"Done. Lines now:\", length(readLines(\"D:/GitHub/kenai-river-wqx/parameters/fecal_coliform.qmd\")))", "intent": "Replace fecal coliform opening paragraph in place" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } }, "databot": { "microCompactKeep": true } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 1327, "cache_read_input_tokens": 75439, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 1327, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 511, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 1327, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null }, "positai": { "timestamp": 1774997143111, "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6", "modelName": "Claude Sonnet 4.6", "providerId": "positai", "usage": { "inputTokens": 1, "outputTokens": 511, "totalTokens": 77278, "cacheReadTokens": 75439, "cacheWriteTokens": 1327, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 1327, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_01PFszGSeiyzoEG86s3cFMkZ", "toolName": "runCode", "output": { "type": "content", "value": [ { "type": "text", "text": "Output:\nDone. Lines now: 50\n\nExecution time: 17ms" } ] }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } }, "databot": { "microCompactKeep": true } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "toolu_013aLUZWdTvvHoGD9ZVihsfC", "toolName": "runCode", "output": { "type": "content", "value": [ { "type": "text", "text": "Output:\n[1] \"Naturally present in many rocks and soils, iron is required by plants and animals for metabolism (Glass, 2001). Sources of detrimental levels of iron are industrial waste, mining, and iron-rich groundwater, and when high concentrations of iron react with dissolved oxygen, precipitates form that can harm salmon eggs and other aquatic life (USEPA, 1976). The ADEC and the USEPA have set the iron standard for the chronic exposure of freshwater aquatic life at 1 mg/L (see Appendix X) (ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 2014).\"\n\n\nExecution time: 5ms" } ] }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } }, "databot": { "microCompactKeep": true } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "reasoning", "text": "Interesting - the file already says \"(USEPA, 1976)\" in the middle of the paragraph for one citation, and \"(ADEC, 2008; USEPA, 2014)\" at the end. { "type": "direct" } }, "databot": { "microCompactKeep": true } } } ] }, { "role": "assistant", "content": [ { "type": "text", "text": "All five changes are done. Here's a brief summary:\n\n**Files changed:**\n\n- **`all_reg_vals.csv`** — 5 new rows added (rows 56–60).\n- **`threshold_table.R`** — added `standard_labels`, `standard_authority`, and `unit_labels` entries for all new standard types, plus `aquatic_life_chronic` which was missing and would have displayed the raw code string in the iron table.\n- **`water_temp.qmd`** — removed the \"Writing in progress\" placeholder; combined the logger/AKTEMP context with the ADEC standards (20/15/13°C) and a note that grab samples should be read alongside continuous records.\n- **`fecal_coliform.qmd`** — rewrote the opening paragraph. The old text explained the 30-day geometric mean criteria at length and then noted they weren't evaluated. The new version leads with the reason (two events per year, not a 30-day sample series), names the single-sample limits as the applicable comparison, and explicitly says an exceedance should prompt additional monitoring rather than be treated as a standard violation.\n- **`iron.qmd`** — `(USEPA, 2014)` → `(USEPA, 1976)`. The 1 mg/L criterion was established in EPA's 1976 \"Quality Criteria for Water\" (Red Book) and has not been revised in EPA's current NRWQC table, so the 1976 date is more accurate.\n\n---\n\n**On the How's My Waterway question (#4):** The 2021 data was uploaded only today, and WQP/HMW propagation typically takes 24–72 hours or longer. That delay is almost certainly the reason the 2021 data isn't visible yet.\n\nFor the historical data not appearing, there are two things worth checking before drawing conclusions:\n\n1. **Does it appear in WQP first?** Go to [waterqualitydata.us](https://www.waterqualitydata.us/), search by Organization ID `KENAI_WQX`, and see if pre-2021 records are returned. If they are, the data exists in WQX but HMW may not have indexed the Kenai watershed. If they aren't, there's a deeper upload gap.\n\n2. **NHD reach mapping.** How's My Waterway displays results organized by NHD waterbody. If the KWF monitoring locations in WQX don't have NHD Reach codes attached, they may not surface in HMW even when the underlying data is in WQP. The `station.csv` as currently structured doesn't include an NHD Reach ID field. Kevin Christian at EPA WQX support would be the right person to ask whether adding NHD codes to the station file would fix HMW visibility.\n\nI'd suggest checking WQP first before assuming there's a pipeline problem — that's the more actionable starting point.\n\nCheck waterqualitydata.us for organization KENAI_WQX to confirm whether historical data is queryable there before investigating HMW-specific issues.\n\nVerify the threshold tables render correctly in the three updated chapters by running a quick test render of those chapters.\n\nCompleted five-file update: water temp/fecal coliform thresholds added to CSV and threshold_table.R; water_temp.qmd, fecal_coliform.qmd, and iron.qmd narratives updated; HMW visibility explained as likely propagation delay plus possible NHD reach mapping gap\n\nUpdated regulatory thresholds and chapter narratives across five files. Added water temperature standards (13/15/20°C, ADEC 18 AAC 70) and fecal coliform single-sample limits (40/400 CFU/100 mL, ADEC) to all_reg_vals.csv and threshold_table.R. Revised water_temp.qmd to integrate the ADEC standards with context about AKTEMP logger data, rewrote the fecal_coliform.qmd opening to clarify that only two samples per year are collected (so the geometric mean criterion cannot be evaluated), and updated the iron.qmd citation from USEPA 2014 to USEPA 1976. Also explained that HMW data visibility issues are likely a propagation delay for the 2021 upload, and suggested checking waterqualitydata.us first to diagnose whether historical data is present in WQX at all." } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 291, "cache_read_input_tokens": 78646, "cache_creation": { "activeChildId": null, "message": { "uiMessage": { "role": "assistant", "content": [ "\n\n
\nThe user wants me to investigate why KWF data doesn't appear in How's My Waterway (HMW). They have a WQP download at `other\\input\\WQX_downloads\\wqp_download_20260331`. Let me first look at the files in that directory, then examine the data to identify potential issues.\n\nKey things to check for HMW visibility:\n1. Activity Type - needs to be a valid type\n2. Monitoring Location identifiers - need to be properly registered\n3. HUC codes - need to be present and correct\n4. NHD Reach codes - may be needed for HMW geographic display\n5. Data structure - one result per activity vs multiple\n\n\n6. State and county codes\n7. Whether the data is actually in WQP first\n\nLet me start by examining what files are in the download directory to see what I'm working with.\n
\n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\nThis is a WQP \"narrow result\" format download. The columns here are the WQP-formatted download, not the original upload format. I notice the column names don't include \"ActivityTypeCode\" directly — it might not be in the narrow result format. Let me look at what's in this download more carefully.\n\nKey things to check for HMW visibility:\n1. What monitoring locations are present\n2. What activity types are used (might be encoded differently in this format)\n3. Whether there's a ResultStatusIdentifier issue\n4. Let me also look at the actual monitoring location IDs and compare with what HMW would need\n\nActually, for HMW to display data, the key requirements are:\n\n\n1. Data must be in WQX (confirmed - they just uploaded)\n2. Monitoring locations must have valid NHD reach codes OR be associated with a waterbody\n3. The data must have \"Accepted\" or \"Final\" status\n\nSo I need to check the ResultStatusIdentifier and monitoring location identifiers. The narrow result format is different from the full WQP download, so I'm looking at what fields are actually available here and what might be causing the visibility issue. Since ActivityTypeCode and ActivityMediaName aren't in this narrow format, I should focus on what's present and whether the monitoring locations have the geographic identifiers HMW needs.\n\nNow I'm thinking through what could prevent HMW from displaying this data. The activity type matters—\"Field Msr/Obs\" should be visible. Result status is critical too; if results are marked as \"Rejected,\" they won't show up. Monitoring location types need to be properly classified, and most importantly, the locations need NHD reach associations or coordinates that can snap to the NHD network, since HMW relies on that geographic framework to position data on the map.\n\nThe HUC code is another consideration—the station data shows HUC8 19020302 for the Kenai watershed, but HMW organizes by HUC12, so there might be a mismatch there. Let me examine the actual monitoring location data and check what the station metadata looks like, particularly the coordinates, HUC codes, and reach codes. The file I'm working with is a narrowresult download with over 16,000 rows, so I should look at what activity type information is included.\n\nThe data is definitely in WQP since this download exists. The real issue for HMW is likely that monitoring locations need to be linked to NHD waterbodies—if the stations don't have NHD reach codes associated with them, HMW won't be able to place them on the map. Let me dig into the station file to see what reach code information is available.\n
\n\nData is in WQP (16,440 rows), so the issue is HMW-specific, not a WQX upload problem. Let me dig into the relevant fields.\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\nThe data is indeed in WQP - 16,440 rows covering 2000-2013 and 2021. \n\nKey observations:\n1. Result Status: \"Final\" (15,605), \"Accepted\" (554), \"Rejected\" (281) — most are \"Final\"\n2. HMW displays data with \"Final\" status, so that's good\n3. Monitoring locations use numeric IDs like \"KENAI_WQX-10000002\"\n\nNow let me look at the monitoring location metadata. The most critical question for HMW is whether the monitoring locations have:\n- NHD Reach codes\n\n\n- Proper coordinates\n- Proper HUC codes\n\nThe narrowresult.csv doesn't have station metadata beyond the ID, so I need to check the station.csv file in the formatted output directory, particularly looking at the HUCEightDigitCode field and whether NHD reach codes are present. For HMW specifically, I'm thinking about how it organizes data by waterbody.\n\nHMW needs NHD waterbody information to place monitoring locations correctly on the map, and if the stations lack NHD Reach Codes, that could be a problem. The activity identifiers like \"KENAI_WQX-10000002\" are just monitoring location IDs, so the real distinguishing factors are the location type and coordinates. Let me examine the station.csv to see if NHDPlus reach codes are actually there.\n
\n\n\n\n
\n\n
\n\n\n\n
\nNow I can see the issue more clearly. The station.csv has:\n- `HUCEightDigitCode`: HUC8 code\n- Coordinates (latitude/longitude)\n- No NHD Reach Code field\n\nFor HMW to display data, it uses NHDPlus to associate monitoring locations with waterbodies. The key field that would help is an **NHD Reach Code** (also called `DrainageAreaMeasure` or `ReachCode` in some WQX versions, or `NHDReachCode` in the full WQX station schema).\n\nActually wait - looking at the WQX station data schema more carefully. WQX's monitoring location schema includes an optional field called `DrainageAreaMeasure` and also `NHDReachCode` (or in the newer WQX 3.0, this might be different).\n \nThe `HorizontalCollectionMethodName` field showing \"Interpolation-Satellite\" could affect how coordinates are interpreted, but the real issue is probably how HMW actually works—it searches by location or waterbody name, displays data by HUC12 subwatersheds, and pulls from WQP for those areas, so the question is whether a user searching for something like \"Kenai River\" would even find this data.\n\nThe core problem: these stations only have HUC8 codes, not HUC12 codes, which means HMW might not be able to locate them when users browse by subwatershed. Let me check what WQP actually returns when I query this organization directly.\n\nI'm looking at the potential blockers—whether the data exists in WQP at all, HMW's refresh schedule, NHD waterbody associations, or Alaska-specific processing issues. The WQX schema should have fields like `DrainageBasinCode` that could help establish the NHD connection.\n\nLet me verify by querying the WQP station service directly to see if these monitoring locations are actually accessible there. The narrowresult.csv confirms the data exists in WQP with thousands of records spanning 2000-2013 and 2021, so the real question is why HMW isn't displaying it—likely either missing NHD reach codes on the monitoring locations themselves, a cache lag in HMW's indexing, or something specific to how Alaska data is processed.\n\nActually, I'm realizing the most probable issue: HMW is built on NHDPlus V2, which only covers the contiguous US and Hawaii, not Alaska. Alaska uses the newer NHDPlus HR, so if HMW hasn't integrated that dataset, it simply can't display any Alaska-based data regardless of whether it exists in WQP.\n
\n\nI see no NHD Reach Code field in the station data, which is potentially significant. Let me check whether HMW's coverage extends to Alaska at all — that may be the core issue.\n\n
\n\n
\n\n
\n\nError making request (HTTP 400): messages.62.content.0: unexpected `tool_use_id` found in `tool_result` blocks: srvtoolu_01CpGmsBJs2ANTnDarhshUiC. The station.csv has:\n- `HUCEightDigitCode`: HUC8 code\n- Coordinates (latitude/longitude)\n- No NHD Reach Code field\n\nFor HMW to display data, it uses NHDPlus to associate monitoring locations with waterbodies. The key field that would help is an **NHD Reach Code** (also called `DrainageAreaMeasure` or `ReachCode` in some WQX versions, or `NHDReachCode` in the full WQX station schema).\n\nActually wait - looking at the WQX station data schema more carefully. WQX's monitoring location schema includes an optional field called `DrainageAreaMeasure` and also `NHDReachCode` (or in the newer WQX 3.0, this might be different).\n \nThe `HorizontalCollectionMethodName` field showing \"Interpolation-Satellite\" could affect how coordinates are interpreted, but the real issue is probably how HMW actually works—it searches by location or waterbody name, displays data by HUC12 subwatersheds, and pulls from WQP for those areas, so the question is whether a user searching for something like \"Kenai River\" would even find this data.\n\nThe core problem: these stations only have HUC8 codes, not HUC12 codes, which means HMW might not be able to locate them when users browse by subwatershed. Let me check what WQP actually returns when I query this organization directly.\n\nI'm looking at the potential blockers—whether the data exists in WQP at all, HMW's refresh schedule, NHD waterbody associations, or Alaska-specific processing issues. The WQX schema should have fields like `DrainageBasinCode` that could help establish the NHD connection.\n\nLet me verify by querying the WQP station service directly to see if these monitoring locations are actually accessible there. The narrowresult.csv confirms the data exists in WQP with thousands of records spanning 2000-2013 and 2021, so the real question is why HMW isn't displaying it—likely either missing NHD reach codes on the monitoring locations themselves, a cache lag in HMW's indexing, or something specific to how Alaska data is processed.\n\nActually, I'm realizing the most probable issue: HMW is built on NHDPlus V2, which only covers the contiguous US and Hawaii, not Alaska. Alaska uses the newer NHDPlus HR, so if HMW hasn't integrated that dataset, it simply can't display any Alaska-based data regardless of whether it exists in WQP.", "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { Let me check whether HMW's coverage extends to Alaska at all — that may be the core issue." }, { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "srvtoolu_01CpGmsBJs2ANTnDarhshUiC", "toolName": "web_search", "input": { "query": "How's My Waterway Alaska coverage NHDPlus limitations" }, "providerExecuted": true }, { "type": "tool-call", "toolCallId": "toolu_01Jz3f5Uiav8Ah2a3UQSo6nn", "toolName": "runCode", "input": { "code": "library(dataRetrieval)\n\n# Pull the WQP station metadata for KENAI_WQX to see what fields are registered\n# This shows what WQP actually has on record for these monitoring locations\nkenai_sites <- whatWQPsites(organization = \"KENAI_WQX\")\ncat(\"Rows:\", nrow(kenai_sites), \"\\n\")\ncat(\"Columns:\", ncol(kenai_sites), \"\\n\\n\")\nprint(names(kenai_sites))", "intent": "Pull monitoring location metadata from WQP to see what fields are registered" }, "providerOptions": { "anthropic": { "caller": { "type": "direct" } }, "databot": { "microCompactKeep": true } } } ], "providerOptions": { "providerMetadata": { "anthropic": { "usage": { "input_tokens": 1, "cache_creation_input_tokens": 1428, "cache_read_input_tokens": 83782, "cache_creation": { "ephemeral_5m_input_tokens": 1428, "ephemeral_1h_input_tokens": 0 }, "output_tokens": 1561, "service_tier": "standard", "inference_geo": "global" }, "cacheCreationInputTokens": 1428, "stopSequence": null, "iterations": null, "container": null, "contextManagement": null }, "positai": { "timestamp": 1774998302778, "modelId": "claude-sonnet-4-6", "modelName": "Claude Sonnet 4.6", "providerId": "positai", "usage": { "inputTokens": 1, "outputTokens": 1561, "totalTokens": 86772, "cacheReadTokens": 83782, "cacheWriteTokens": 1428, "cacheWrite5mTokens": 1428, "cacheWrite1hTokens": 0 } } } } }, { "role": "tool", "content": [ { "type": "tool-result", "toolCallId": "srvtoolu_01CpGmsBJs2ANTnDarhshUiC", "toolName": "web_search", "output": { "type": "text", "value": "[Tool executed by provider -
